I apologize. I didn't give very good advice, as I was working from memory.

What I meant was to run

```
python -c "import lib2to3.pygram"
```

That will trigger the Python environment to generate the lib2to3 grammar, which it stores as a pickle. If that code gets triggered first as part of installing a package, it will cause that sandbox violation (because writing outside of the sandboxed directory is disallowed).

My guess is if you run the command above, the install will work. It's possible you don't even need to run the command above and that it'll work. I'm not sure why installing httpie works but installing irc doesn't. It could be because irc uses 'setup_requires' requirements.

In any case, it's not a bug with irc. Please let me know if the above command doesn't get things going.

Your runner will be passed a reference to the HTTPTestCase object, from there you can get its name in I think two different ways:

* `test_case.test_data['name']`
* `test_case.__class__.__name__`

Would accessing those from your runner (using them to assign something) help? If you have a look at [gabbi.reporter](https://github.com/cdent/gabbi/blob/master/gabbi/reporter.py) it has some related examples.

The `test_request` thing is an artifact of the way in which metaclasses were necessary to manage the ordered listing between test cases. This means that each chunk of yaml is a TestCase instance rather than a test method inside a TestCase. I tried to do the latter but the it was too messy to do the ordering.

I've seen the problem you're describing also with one of the reporting tools in OpenStack CI and it surprises me because it appears to be making the assumption that test method names are supposed to be unique across an entire test suite, rather than unique within their class.

Seems like it might make sense to turn off the `Attempt to flush stdout after each command is run` option if running remotely. I'm hesitant to turn it off unconditionally because program output often does not show up until the program exits. Does that sounds like a good solution to you?

The root of the problem is that gdb is being run as a subprocess of python and not attached directly to a terminal, so the programs being debugged have their output buffered by the kernel. Using something like https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js would fix the root of the problem, though that is a significant change to the architecture of gdbgui.

However... even if the history can be easily loaded inside `_init_pdb()`, I didn't find a way to store it in a file because it has to be done after pdb console exit.

Is there a way to launch a command after pdb console exit, without changing the user experience?

If for example `set_trace()` is used, the executions breaks in the line that calls it, but there's no way to do something after the end of the debugging session because the scope returns on the main program and not on ipdb code.

This is possibly a bug in the specification. I checked with the function name `glGetIntegeri_v`. Looking it up in the XML it is defined in the 3.0 section and in the 3.1 section.

```xml
    <feature api="gl" name="GL_VERSION_3_0" number="3.0">
        <require>
[...]
            <command name="glGetBooleani_v"/>
            <command name="glGetIntegeri_v"/>
            <command name="glEnablei"/>
[...]
        </require>
    </feature>
    <feature api="gl" name="GL_VERSION_3_1" number="3.1">
[...]
            <command name="glBindBufferBase"/>
            <command name="glGetIntegeri_v"/>
        </require>
    </feature>
```

If you want to report the bug you can open an issue here: https://github.com/KhronosGroup/OpenGL-Registry

I'm working on the mpc API and I think I spotted a bug in GMPy_MPFR_New(). Can you add a Cython test that uses a precision of 0? A precision of 0 should cause gmpy2 to use the precision of the currently active context. I suspect it may crash in the current gmpy2 code.

Note that contexts have subtly, but significantly, changed in version 2.1. In gmpy2 2.0.x, there was only a single global context that was shared by all thread. Contexts are now thread specific.

Couple of small questions. I'm not sure about naming the API calls properly for a straightforward and self-explanatory use, so @ureg.require_units ? @ureg.require_dimensions ? 

Second, in my usage and the gist i posted, i do not enforce or check return value there ( different from @ureg.wraps ). My reasoning is this : if i'm writing a library that manipulates quantities and it expects inputs from unknown code, checking their dimensionality attribute is probably the least offensive type checking, as exact type checking in kind of un-pythonic. If the duck has a dimension of length, you can reasonably try to calculate how many of them fit in the phone booth.

However, returning correct type from the same library call should be ensured by the unit tests on my code, rather than at run-time by the wrapper function. 

The reasoning is different for @ureg.wraps having a return type spec, as it is intended to actually perform conversions on a wrapped function. Does that make sense ?

Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

>>> str(icmp)

Note that this is an IP header that encapsulated in an ICMP error message, by RFC it should be truncated at 28 byte while the original IP packet may be longer. Calling str() will incorrectly change the ```len``` field in the encapsulated IP header.

`thefuck` actually has a long way to go regarding whitespace/metachar-safety. Take 237f43e as an example (I found the cd issue when searching for 'whitespace' in issues to make sure that I didn't get a dup):

```Python
return 'cd "{0}"'.format(cwd)
```

This is unfortunately not the safe thing to do, e.g.:
```sh
cd "$(mktemp -d)"
mkdir aaaa\"
thefuck cd aaa
```
You will get some output like:
```sh
cd "/tmp/tmp.R71CQrZT1O/aaaa"" 
```
Unbalanced quotes.

The Right Thing :tm: to do is to actually use lists as the data structure for internal representation of single commands, i.e.
```Python
return ['cd', cwd]
```
And finally do some quoting based on shell-dependent rules before outputting:
```Python
# e.g. POSIX-ish shells:
def quote(self, list):
    return ' '.join("'{0}'".format(arg.replace("'", "'\\''")) for arg in list) # shlex.quote(arg)
```

Okay, okay, you made me look.

I'll give you points for trying, and I'll give you a _lot_ of points for working code.  A working starting point is precious.

But PyPDF2-style code usually pretty much misses the entire point of how pdfrw works, so I've taken your working code and munged it into something that feels better to me.  I didn't spend a lot of time on it, so there might be some rough edges.

Also, as I mentioned earlier, I may not be really keen on adding too much more stuff to pdfwriter.py.  For example, I could see adding a generic write-hook that a bookmark module could use to hook itself in, so then we could have a bookmarks.py that has a function you specifically invoke to use bookmarks.

I haven't really thought about it that much, though, so for now, I kept your basic structure of subclassing the writer -- except for how the Info dict is handled.  One of the things that makes the PyPDF2 codebase unwieldy is the proliferation of java-style getters and setters.  pdfrw is not big on that -- if you want to set up an Info dict, then just set up an Info dict.  Otherwise, where does it stop?

newwriter.py:

```
from pdfrw.pdfwriter import PdfWriter, IndirectPdfDict, PdfName, \
                            PdfOutputError, PdfDict, user_fmt


class NewPdfWriter(PdfWriter):

    _outline = None

    def addBookmark(self, title, pageNum, parent=None):
        '''
        Adds a new bookmark entry.
        pageNum must be a valid page number in the writer
        and parent can be a bookmark object returned by
        a previous addBookmark call
        '''

        try:
            page = self.pagearray[pageNum]
        except IndexError:
            # TODO: Improve error handling ?
            PdfOutputError("Invalid page number: " % (pageNum))

        parent = parent or self._outline
        if parent is None:
            parent = self._outline = IndirectPdfDict()

        bookmark = IndirectPdfDict(
            Parent = parent,
            Title = title,
            A = PdfDict(
                D = [page, PdfName.Fit],
                S = PdfName.GoTo
            )
        )

        if parent.Count:
            parent.Count += 1
            prev = parent.Last
            bookmark.Prev = prev
            prev.Next = bookmark
            parent.Last = bookmark
        else:
            parent.Count = 1
            parent.First = bookmark
            parent.Last = bookmark

        return bookmark


    def write(self, fname, trailer=None, user_fmt=user_fmt,
              disable_gc=True):
        trailer = trailer or self.trailer
        trailer.Root.Outlines = self._outline
        super(NewPdfWriter, self).write(fname, trailer, user_fmt, disable_gc)
```

I checked the code in dev (6a3131742f13c5873f0deb6de4acd2166a6e790c), and indeed, the AD search script from above works just fine! Moreover, all three alternative formats for specifying the objectGUID work, i.e.

* ``(objectGUID=\2c\4f\20\73\49\b9\39\4c\a3\ae\79\fb\51\19\e6\76)``
* ``(objectGUID=2c4f2073-49b9-394c-a3ae79fb5119e676)``

all give the same result. That's great!

I also checked RFC 4122 and MS-DTYP. My two cents:
* I think the curly-braces big-endian UUID notation (``{73204f2c-b949-4c39-a3ae-79fb5119e676}``) is just fine, as specified in MS-DTYP 2.3.4.3 and the RFC. I also like that ``format_uuid_le`` returns this notation now, as this allows to directly re-use attribute values in filter strings.
* Likewise, I think the little-endian LDAP byte notation (``\2c\4f\20\73\49\b9\39\4c\a3\ae\79\fb\51\19\e6\76``) works as specified.
* However, I couldn't find anything in MS-DTYP or the RFC regarding the just-dashes little-endian UUID notation (``2c4f2073-49b9-394c-a3ae79fb5119e676``). I probably overlooked something, but as this notation and the little-endian LDAP byte notation are pretty much interchangeable, I'm not sure if ldap3 needs to support it?

> should i runDispatcher() again for third request 

Typically, you run main loop (runDispatcher() just once at the end of your script), the main loop is a blocking call e.g. it might only return when no pending requests left. All the live happens in your callback functions being triggered by external events.

You can make `runDispatcher()` running forever or make it running just for as long as you have at least one request not answered/timed out. In the latter case you will have `runDispatcher()` returned so you would have to re-start `runDispatcher()` once get some new queries to process.

I'd propose instead of tuples (`(1, 2)`), you take arrays (`numpy.array([1, 2])`), perhaps with an enforced `dtype=int`. Those should be seamlessly readable and writable by other formats as well, and it would be nice if this info doesn't get lost in format translation.

The only thing that you have to take care of then is in `gmsh_io.write()` to only admit field arrays of type `int` and length `2`, and to issue a warning if you're skipping a field.

It is now possible to define a `save_threshold` value as keyword in the algorithm, e.g. like this:
```
from spotpy.examples.spot_setup_rosenbrock import spot_setup
sampler=spotpy.algorithms.mc(spot_setup(), 
dbname='RosenMC', dbformat='csv', save_threshold=-1000)
sampler.sample(5000)
results = sampler.getdata()
```

It is also possible to compare a list of objectivefunctions with a list of save_threshold values:
```
def objecttivefuntion(self,simulation,evaluation,params=None):
        objf1 = spotpy.objectivefunctions.r_squared(evaluation = evaluation, simulation = simulation)  
        objf2 = spotpy.objectivefunctions.rmse(evaluation = evaluation, simulation = simulation)
        return [objf1,objf2] 
```

And for the thresholds:
```
sampler=spotpy.algorithms.mc(spot_setup(), 
dbname='RosenMC', dbformat='csv', save_threshold=[0.7,200)
```

A bit more in favor of double-octave representations:

- It's true that no current metric captures the distinction between `C:9` and `C:7(2)`, and yeah, the latter is kind of a garbage chord.  But it might be useful to have the ability to capture extensions in the future -- I'm all about making new metrics if our current ones are broken. :grin: 
- OTOH, if you're comparing a `C:9` to ` C:sus2` (on, say, the mirex metric), I think it's reasonable to treat the 2 and the 9 as distinct, and not count them as a pitch class hit.  Then again, I'm not a music theorist, so feel free to shoot me down on that.
- Having an explicit representation for extensions would make it easier to invert encodings back to chord labels.
- There's no harm in implementing it and leaving it disabled by default, or implementing the metrics so that they only look at the first twelve dimensions unless you set an extension flag to `True`.

> delivers 0 for the NCE over-segmentation score, although it should be 1.

I agree that it's a little counter-intuitive when the reference and estimates are identical, but NCE is actually undefined in the cases you list above due to a factor of `1/log(1)` (see the original [paper](http://ismir2008.ismir.net/papers/ISMIR2008_219.pdf), section 3).  This is also covered briefly in the mir_eval [paper](http://bmcfee.github.io/papers/ismir2014_mireval.pdf), section 4.6.

We made a conscious decision to differ from MIREX and take the 0 convention in mir_eval.  I'll try to dig up the thread where we hashed this out.

#!/usr/bin/env python

"""
Example from pybedtools documentation (:ref:`third example`) to count \
reads in introns and exons using multiple CPUs.
"""

from __future__ import print_function
import pybedtools
import argparse
import os
import sys
import multiprocessing


def featuretype_filter(feature, featuretype):
    """
    Only passes features with the specified *featuretype*
    """
    if feature[2] == featuretype:
        return True
    return False


def subset_featuretypes(featuretype):
    return g.filter(featuretype_filter, featuretype).saveas()


def count_reads_in_features(features):
    """
    Callback function to count reads in features
    """
    return features.intersect(abam=bam,
                             b=features.fn,
                             s=stranded,
                             bed=True,
                             stream=True).count()


def main():
    """
    Third quick example from the documentation -- count reads introns and
    exons, in parallel
    """
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog=os.path.basename(sys.argv[0]),
                                 usage=__doc__)
    ap.add_argument('--gff', required=True,
                    help='GFF or GTF file containing annotations')
    ap.add_argument('--bam', required=True,
                    help='BAM file containing reads to be counted')
    ap.add_argument('--stranded', action='store_true',
                    help='Use strand-specific merging and overlap. '
                         'Default is to ignore strand')
    ap.add_argument('--no-parallel', dest='noparallel', action='store_true',
                    help='Disables parallel computation')
    ap.add_argument('-o', '--output',
                    help='Optional file to which results will be written; '
                         'default is stdout')
    ap.add_argument('-v', '--verbose', action='store_true',
                    help='Verbose (goes to stderr)')
    args = ap.parse_args()

    gff = args.gff
    bam = args.bam
    stranded = args.stranded
    parallel = not args.noparallel

    # Some GFF files have invalid entries -- like chromosomes with negative
    # coords or features of length = 0.  This line removes them and saves the
    # result in a tempfile
    g = pybedtools.BedTool(gff).remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Decide which version of map to use.  If parallel, we only need 3
    # processes.
    pool = multiprocessing.Pool(processes=3)

    # Get separate files for introns and exons in parallel (if specified)
    featuretypes = ('intron', 'exon')
    introns, exons = pool.map(subset_featuretypes, featuretypes)

    # Perform some genome algebra to get unique and shared regions
    exon_only = exons.subtract(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_only = introns.subtract(exons).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()
    intron_and_exon = exons\
            .intersect(introns).merge().remove_invalid().saveas()

    # Do intersections with BAM file in parallel
    features = (exon_only, intron_only, intron_and_exon)
    results = pool.map(count_reads_in_features, features)

    labels = ('      exon only:',
              '    intron only:',
              'intron and exon:')

    for label, reads in zip(labels, results):
        print('%s %s' % (label, reads))

    pybedtools.cleanup(verbose=False)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
`

Is this definitely a bug? Generally TypeDecls associated with declarators have the identifier's name associated with them in the declname, and the CGenerator uses that. `
func_type = ast.ext[-3].type.type
cast_type = PtrDecl(func_type.quals, TypeDecl(None, func_type.type.quals, func_type.type.type))
cg.visit(Cast(cast_type, FuncCall(ID('test_fun'), ExprList([]))))
`

This gives the expected output:
`(void *) test_fun()`

(There's also a theoretical risk for server software that takes all files (key, cert, intermediates) in one file, like haproxy: What if an attacker replaces the issuer certificate with a private key? Ok, I know why the assertion is in place.

```python
    validators = {}
    if '$ref' in schema:
        validators['$ref'] = LazyReferenceValidator(
            schema['$ref'],
            context,
        )
    if 'properties' in schema:
        # I don't know why this set intersection is enforced...?  Why did I do this.
        intersection = set(schema['properties'].keys()).intersection(schema.keys())
        assert not intersection

        for property_, property_schema in schema['properties'].items():
            property_validators = construct_schema_validators(
                property_schema,
                context,
            )
            validators[property_] = skip_if_empty(skip_if_not_of_type(OBJECT)(
                chain_reduce_partial(
                    operator.methodcaller('get', property_, EMPTY),
                    functools.partial(validate_object, validators=property_validators),
                ),
            ))
```

This function begins by creating a dictionary (`validators`) and then assigning validation function to keys in that dictionary.  The assertion that the property keys do not intersect with the schema keys ensures that we don't overwrite any of the primary schema validators with those from the property validators.  I'm assuming that in your full schema you both define a `type` for your schema as well as it having a `type` property.

This is a shortcoming of using a plain old dictionary to store the validation functions.

This limitation is fixed in https://github.com/pipermerriam/flex/pull/42 which is still in progress.  I've been slowly chipping away at the refactor but it's a big one and is taking time.

Hi @1nickt!

The scroller widget is my least favorite widget. It does a lot of DOM manipulation to wrap the table, add duplicate tables & make all the cells line up. This makes the widget very inefficient and is really slow on large tables.

I really wouldn't recommend using it if you only wanted to freeze a column.

Complaining aside. It sounds like the build-in box-sizing might be overridden in your example. The table and all components inside the scroller have the following css applied:

```css
.tablesorter-scroller * { box-sizing: border-box; }
```

If this gets changed by other css, it make really mess up alignment. So double check to make sure this isn't happening.

If that doesn't appear to be the issue, please modify [this demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/mqv16pd4/) to duplicate the problem to help me better troubleshoot this issue.

Hi, thanks for opening the issue, I think the problem there is that you are applying a `max_left_rotation` of `50` and that may well result in a malformed image due to the way Augmentor performs cropping on the rotated images by default. 

This is because Augmentor rotates and then extracts the maximum crop possible of the same dimensions as the input image, and depending on the dimensions of this input image and the rotation applied, you may not get valid images as output.

Take a look at [http://augmentor.readthedocs.io/en/master/code.html#Augmentor.Pipeline.Pipeline.rotate](http://augmentor.readthedocs.io/en/master/code.html#Augmentor.Pipeline.Pipeline.rotate).
Thank you!

Actually if I see well the "resolving_decorator" included in that package, it's because imho it's just a variant of the "decorator.decorator" utility. 

Below is an excerpt from the decorator documentation. 

```
>>> @decorator
... def trace(f, *args, **kw):
... kwstr = ', '.join('%r: %r' % (k, kw[k]) for k in sorted(kw))
... print("calling %s with args %s, {%s}" % (f.__name__, args, kwstr))
... return f(*args, **kw)
```

As you see, when the decorated function get called at runtime, all arguments are passed as standard "*args, **kw" pair. So inside this "trace" decorator, it's complicated to extract, for example, an argument which would be an SQL session, because one doesn't know if it's in "args" (passed by position) or "kwargs" (passed by keyword).

The "resolving_decorator" would allow "trace" to receive a single "**all_kwargs" data set, with all arguments already bound to their final "local variable" name. Thus "trace" could simply do all_kwargs.get("sql_session"), and fiddle with this pre-resolved parameter. The only constraint would be, of course, that all decorated functions which want to have sql session handling, expose a "sql_session" argument in their signature.

Okay, so let me see if I understand the behavior you're looking for. Here's what I think you're asking for:

Insert an image into a picture placeholder, such that:

* the image is completely enclosed by the placeholder
* the aspect ratio of the image is preserved

You found yourself in mostly unlikely scenario as you are the first one to test a string with brackets. Most placeholders will "never" have brackets so it's not worth changing.

```python
import sc2
from sc2 import run_game, maps, Race, Difficulty, position
from sc2.player import Bot, Computer
from sc2.constants import NEXUS, PROBE, PYLON

class TestBot(sc2.BotAI):
    def __init__(self):
        self.moved_workers = False
        self.got_idle = False

    async def on_step(self, iteration):
        if not self.moved_workers:
            self.moved_workers = True
            # get position
            top_cen = self.game_info.map_ramps[0].top_center
            go_to = position.Point2(position.Pointlike(top_cen))

            # get workers
            workers = self.units(PROBE)
            for w in workers:
                await self.do(w.move(go_to))

        if iteration > 4500 and not self.got_idle:
            self.got_idle = True
            # get position
            top_cen = self.game_info.map_ramps[1].top_center
            go_to = position.Point2(position.Pointlike(top_cen))

            # get idle workers
            workers = self.units(PROBE).idle
            for w in workers:
                await self.do(w.move(go_to))

            # start a probe just to be sure of when frame 4500 occurs
            for nexus in self.units(NEXUS).ready:
                if self.can_afford(PROBE):
                    await self.do(nexus.train(PROBE))


run_game(maps.get("NeonVioletSquareLE"), [
    Bot(Race.Protoss, TestBot()),
    Computer(Race.Terran, Difficulty.Easy)
], realtime=True)
```

`pretty_midi` discards the track name, because it collapses tracks into instruments by program number.  So, for example, in your GarageBand screenshot, tracks MELODY and SQ-R will be put into the same `pretty_midi.Instrument` because they have the same program number.  There are various reasons for this; I'd be happy to go into it if you want.

It should be possible to keep track of any `midi.TrackNameEvent`s while populating the instruments (the method `pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI._load_instruments`) and store them in the `Instruments`, maybe in a list or something.  I have not added this functionality, mainly because it is not directly compatible with `pretty_midi`'s representation of MIDI instruments, but moreso because in my experience relatively few MIDI files "in the wild" make use of track name events, and when they are used there's no standardization (unlike, say, program numbers thanks to general MIDI).

 - Having only a single client can lead to crashes and being unable to handle unpair requests.
 - Replies and updates are delivered to the last connected client.
 - accessory.pickle is not updated/deleted on unpair.

> Can you confirm what is the best to trade LIVE with the same strategy on multiple preselected symbols ?

Although the code in the 2 live data feeds is theoretically prepared for that, it has only been tested with a single symbol. It may even break with more than one.

Having *many symbols* and scanning for signals and choosing one seems to be a topic for many, but it is not really the focus of *backtrader*.

Rather than having an internal loop which kicks in every *x period-seconds* to synchronize the data received by feeds, *backtrader* features a *master data* and *slaves datas*.

The system blocks on the the *master* data until it receives something from it. The other datas are then queried for data. In the case of pre-canned data this is a perfect way to synchronize data from different sources.

This *master-slave* approach makes the system inherently biased to single data unless the *slave-datas* can produce data in the background, before being queried. This is theoretically the approach of the live datas, but it would need to be tested.

Until then it wouldn't be recommended to try it with more than 1 data.

A simple solution is to apply [`os.path.realpath`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.realpath) on all watched folders. So, we end up with something like:

    observer.schedule(event_handler, os.path.realpath(path))

- `errno.EACCES` is `13`

The `watchdog.observers.kqueue` and `watchdog.observers.fsevents`also import the `DirectorySnapshot` for some reason. I'm not sure if removing that bypass of `errno.ENOENT` ("No Such Entity", which is raised when trying to list a directory that doesn't exist, e.g. when it had been removed in the middle of the private `walk` recursion happening in the `DirectorySnapshot.__init__` process) won't harm anyone. Unless I'm missing something, that exception handling probably should have had been kept as is (it's independent from the remaining changes).

For changing what had already been done, please do that by rebasing/squashing/amending the commits instead of adding more commits, in order to have a slightly cleaner history (I was thinking on writing a full changelog for this project, but the current history doesn't help).

(Perhaps 2 instead of 3, since I'm not sure if the *unmount and mount* issue would work the same way here, as polling an unmounted path should behave as a common directory in the parent directory's file system, not as the previously mounted file system).

Of course it is caused by the recursion in Node.from_intervals() - that's the way the tree is constructed, and it seems that in your case the construction code is not doing the balancing job correctly.

Unfortunately, reproducing the bug by just picking 40k random intervals does not work. E.g. this code works fine on my machine:

    from intervaltree import IntervalTree, Interval
    import cPickle as pickle
    import os,sys,random

    random.seed(1)
    def genintervals():
        for i in range(40000):
            a, b, c, d = [random.randint(1, 1000) for j in range(4)]
            yield min(a, b), max(a, b)+1, (str(c), str(d))

    tree = IntervalTree()
    for a, b, d in genintervals():
        tree.addi(a, b, d)

    tree = pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(tree))

    print len(tree)

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
@dataclassclass Wrapper:

I assumed I could use it like:

instance = Wrapper(1)

from typing import Any
@dataclassclass Wrapper:
    value: Any = field()

unannotated attributes on a dataclass decorated declaration that are
instances of dataclasses.Field would be treated as fields on the data class.

Here's what you gotta do: use callable rate values
http://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/rates.html#callables.

The problem is that you'll need to write some callable that can do the job.
If it's a fixed IP address you know in advance, maybe that's easy.
Otherwise, you'll need to put something into some non-volatile data store
to read and say "oh if it's this IP, return 1000/h, otherwise return 20/h".

Providing a batteries-included implementation of this is the goal of #52
https://github.com/jsocol/django-ratelimit/issues/52, though I admit I
don't have an ETA or anything on that. There's a very, very incomplete
example there that may be a useful starting point.

That is a really interesting approach! I hadn’t heard of it before. The Stripe post was really great, too, just as a resource. They didn’t describe their implementation, but I did find one from [ClassDojo](https://engineering.classdojo.com/blog/2015/02/06/rolling-rate-limiter/). 

This algorithm is definitely more complex and I don’t think it’s a good fit for a default because I don’t see how you could avoid the race condition issues with Memcached—or generally without using methods not available through the Django cache interface. That would tightly couple ratelimit to Redis and I don’t think that’s OK. (I am happy to be proven wrong, though, if someone can describe a race-condition-free variant using only Django cache API methods.)

A long time ago there was a concept of pluggable backends that was removed in favor of relying directly and purely on the cache API. I still generally believe that’s the right call for this library, and in particular I’m not sure how the token bucket approach would work with the current `@ratelimit` decorator API. But I think there’s definitely room for more libraries out there! Or if you want to open another issue with a proposal, please do!

What do you think about the following approach? It should only take two queries if I understand things correctly:

```py
# reversion/revisions.py:490

...

# Save version models.
for version in new_versions:
    version.revision = revision
# query #1: Bulk save (does not return versions with primary keys)
Version.objects.using(db).bulk_create(new_versions)
# query #2: replace new_versions with filtered set from db
# which will include the primary keys
new_versions = Version.objects.filter(revision=revision)

...

# Send the post_revision_commit signal.
post_revision_commit.send(self,
    instances = ordered_objects,
    revision = revision,
    versions = new_versions,
)

...
```

Although I would assume that most of the time a revision will only have one version if people are just using it with the out of the box admin integration. So if you really wanted to optimize, you could check the length of the versions list and if it's less than a certain threshold, save them individually, otherwise bulk save them then retrieve them.

The problem is in VersionQuerySet.get_deleted method. It generates a very slow query to get deleted items, and the slowest part of it is (I will keep the names of my tables):

```SELECT MAX(V0."id") AS "latest_pk" FROM "reversion_version" V0 WHERE (V0."db" = 'default' AND V0."content_type_id" = 216 AND NOT (V0."object_id" IN (SELECT CAST(U0."id" as text) AS "id_str" FROM "podblogs_post" U0))) GROUP BY V0."object_id"```

This query has a sub-query to get a list of items that are in `reversion_version` table but not in `podblogs_post`. I have about 200.000 records in my tables so this query runs almost 40 minutes on my Core i7 laptop. 
But getting a list of delete items is much more simple with JOIN:

```SELECT MAX(V."id") AS "latest_pk" FROM "reversion_version" V 
LEFT JOIN "podblogs_post" P ON CAST(V."object_id" AS INT)=P."id" WHERE V.db='default' AND V."content_type_id"=216 AND P."id" IS NULL GROUP BY V."object_id";
 ```
This query returns the same results as the one above and runs 300 ms only compared to 40 minutes.
So in my project I decided to override `recoverlist_view` method and replace `get_deleted` method with a raw SQL query:

```
        deleted = Version.objects.raw(
        '''
        SELECT * FROM "reversion_version"
        WHERE (
           "reversion_version"."db" = '{1}' AND "reversion_version"."content_type_id" = {0} AND "reversion_version"."id" IN (
              SELECT MAX(V."id") AS "latest_pk" FROM "reversion_version" V
              LEFT JOIN "podblogs_post" P ON CAST(V."object_id" AS INT)=P."id" WHERE V.db='{1}' AND V."content_type_id"={0} AND P."id" IS NULL GROUP BY V."object_id"
            )
        )
        ORDER BY "reversion_version"."id" ASC
        '''.format(content_type.id, Version.objects.db)
        )
```
Now `Recover deleted posts` page opens instantly. You can apply similar query to your model admin.

You can just add that to your own view logic. get_for_object_reference() just returns a queryset, so you can apply whatever optimizations you require to it.

Same problem for me: delimiter and quotechar keywordargs for `DictReader()` and `Reader()` have to be string, not unicode. This is kind of anoying when you want to work only with unicode...
Workaround: 
```python
delimiter=str(u';'), quotechar=str(u'"')
```
Here the u prefix is just for clarity, most of the time my 2.7 module have `from __future__ import unicode_literals` to ensure Python 3 compatibility (and to ensure correct utf8 handling everywhere).

I'd like to reopen this issue, because the implementation of the binary-to-hexadecimal conversion is not interoperable with other implementations of the same algorithm, and simply not interoperable with "standard" value-conversion functions.

Therefore, if I calculate the pHash with `imagehash` and then I take its hex value and interpret it with another "standard" hex-to-bin function, I obtain a completely different value.

When I say "standard" I mean something like "widely used". Take for example the `bitstring.BitArray` class:

```
In [3]: BitArray(bin='1000111100001111000011110000111100001111000010110000101101111010')
Out[3]: BitArray('0x8f0f0f0f0f0b0b7a')
```

versus

```
In [13]: _binary_array_to_hex(np.array([bool(int(x)) for x in '1000111100001111000011110000111100001111000010110000101101111010']))
Out[13]: 'f1f0f0f0f0d0d05e'
```

Again, for Hamming-distance comparison, as long as the bits are the same, it doesn't matter. However, if you use other libraries, you might get weird results.

Hi @kevex91, thanks for the submission. I think you did a great job with the code and there's nothing wrong with your pull request. I encourage you to keep submitting PRs! My only suggestion in the future would be to add test cases along with any new code you add because most people won't accept new code without test cases to go with it.

But the real question is if this is a feature that this library needs. I want to keep the API for this library really simple so it's easy to learn. There's no right or wrong answer to whether or not this library should have this function and it's totally a personal preference thing.

In my opinion, adding a structured response like this is kind of heavy. It would be faster and simpler for someone to just call `numpy.argmin(face_distance(known_face_encodings, face_encoding_to_check)))` to get the face with the lowest distance. The overhead of wrapping everything in a dictionary isn't really needed and would slow things down (especially processing video or something time-sensitive).

But again, that's just my opinion. I don't mean to say that anything is wrong with your code and someone else might have a different take :) But my preference would be not to add an extra function for this. But anyone using the library could easily grab your code and use it in their own program if they prefer it to work that way.

For the Travis-CI (also applies to something like TeamCity) use-case that I am referencing, I need to have `caniusepython3` exit with a non-zero code so that the test does not pass when there are dependencies that are not compatible with python3. That way as PRs come along in a project, a change that breaks compatibility with python3 via dependencies will incur a visible test failure.

Global sequence alignment uses Needleman-Wunsch algorithm. Local sequence alignment uses Smith-Waterman algorithm.

About your blockers:

* It does give you the original elements if you decode the aligned sequences. Check out the final loop in the example. As for the indices, you're right; it doesn't give you the original indices. However, you can pair your elements with their indices and write a custom similarity function that ignores the indices when comparing the elements. Then, when you decode, you should get back the original indices.

* I was a little bit obsessed about generating all possible alignments when I wrote the backtracing algorithm. Hence the recursion. It should be pretty easy to write an iterative, constant space backtracer that stops when it founds one solution.

I will keep the issue open so I can address the lack of documentation and the iterative backtracing when/if I find time. But please feel free to contribute if you have the motivation.

i *think* the issue is that when enumerating chunks (and therefore records) via the `Evtx` log object, the log object doesn't take into account how many chunks are declared to be in the log file. Instead, it keeps reading until the end of the file. So, if there's junk data appended to the file, it attempts to parse this as valid data. See here: https://github.com/williballenthin/python-evtx/blob/5fb7662506d2c39aee5a9a143a9cc47d3bdc461d/Evtx/Evtx.py#L92

Hi Anton and thanks for the feedback.

> Could you please clarify why we don't want to keep the most recent one, and remove the one made at 21:15?

I guess it's just a preference :-). When I originally developed `rotate-backups` I made the decision to preserve the first (oldest) backup in each time slot. For my use cases I don't consider it relevant to prefer more recent backups over less recent backups because my focus is on long term archival (versus e.g. disaster recovery).

> Does it make sense to add some option (like `--keep-recent`) to change this behaviour and keep the latest backups?

I just released version 4.1 which adds support for the `-p`, `--prefer-recent` command line option. I've also updated the documentation to explain what the default rotation algorithm is and how to change it.

> As a general (and somehow related) question, is it planned to introduce a minutely option to enable rotation if backups are made several times per hour?

I just released version 4.2 which adds support for the `-M`, `--minutely` command line option :-).

@a-sk I think it would be problematic if somebody issues a chmod on `dst` (manually, outside of python-atomicwrites), and some other program uses python-atomicwrites at the same time:

* python-atomicwrites gets `stat` of `dst`.
* Somebody changes permissions of `dst`.
* python-atomicwrites restores the *old* permissions on `src`.
* python-atomicwrites calls `rename`, restores the *old* permissions on `dst`.

You have similar problems even if you restore the permissions after you `rename`d. So restoring permissions and `renaming` should happen in one [atomic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atomicity_%28database_systems%29) step.

First, thanks for such a useful library. 

Second, thanks for being responsive to my concerns!

Third, with respect, developers will want to do what they want to do, and so deciding for them what is a bad idea is IMHO counter-productive.  They will simply fork to accomplish their goal (see #1 -- thank you for even making that possible!  :-). 

With that, let me explain my use case more fully.  I am producing files server-side that follow a particular naming convention that is based in part on the resource's name and PK (via a class passed to `upload_to`).  The users download them and then use them for importing into other programs.  With consistency naming the users can then just hit the endpoint directly without needing to know anything other than the meta data of the resource itself. Obviously this form of discovery will not be possible when random characters are appended to the file.

Secondly, files in cloudinary are not overwritten and aren't garbage-collected using the `deleteorphanmedia` command.  Perhaps this is just a bug, but the net effect is that I get a lot of cruft that not only increases storage costs but makes management difficult.

When using the native CloudinaryField from pycloudinary, all this was handled.  I could rename the file, which would overwrite the reference to the latest version (through the `public_id`) and then keep versioned copies of prior uploads.  I am not sure how cacheing would impact this, but it seemed to work flawlessly for me.  However, I couldn't use the native FileField tools via Django and found your approach compelling.  But I need to consider the tradeoffs and might need to go back.

What might be happening here is a disconnect between using a filename v. using a public_id.  The `public_id` seems to abstract some of these problems away, leaving the management to cloudinary (which is good that's what I pay them for).    Does that make sense?

@adamcrussell the 'data' is a default name for the variable where we expect the data to come from into the net.
If you use different name then you should create a module with additional parameters.
In this case use:
```
my $module = mx->mod->Module(symbol => $softmax, data_names => ['data_age']);
```
If you have more than one input variable then specify them all in the data_names array.
Also do not forget to have your iterator also return AI::MXNet::DataDesc with the correct names and shapes (the 'provide_data') call.

@bolkedebruin the `timestamp` column is calling the utcnow() in the XCom class attributes, only once, resulting in all xcoms having the exact same timestamp.

Use something like this
```
    timestamp = Column(
        DateTime(timezone=True), default=timezone.utcnow, nullable=False)
```

@ashb raising the default `sql_alchemy_pool_size` to 15 also seemed to help reduce the database load for us. The default pool size of 5 seems to be much to low for airflow, and causes a lot of connections to be opened/closed, which could have stayed in the pool to be reused.

I think the idea of this PR is definitely correct and if there are load issues that block this PR from being merged they should be addressed (e.g. if we need to throttle or prioritize dagruns between different DAGs that should be addressed regardless of this PR). Can you try setting

    cmd_options = {

I've thought about this for a while and I think I'm going to pass on it for `.clean()`.

There are two big reasons for doing this:

1. `.clean()` is about removing malicious content--not about transforming HTML documents for other mediums or prettifying content. `.clean()` is a security-focused function and as such, keeping its functionality minimal reduces the likelihood of bugs that have security-related impact. That's really important.
2. Seems like the use cases for stripping content in tags are related to transforming HTML documents and prettifying content. I think that should get handled by a different function or possibly not by bleach at all.

Towards that, I've clarified the goals/non-goals language and updated the docs to make it clearer what `.clean()` is supposed to be doing and what it's not going to be good for.

Given that, I'm going to pass on this and close it out.

I'm game for talking about building a `.prettify()` function which is about prettifying HTML which would cover the use these changes cover. That should be a new issue, though.

This is by design. There are some particular reasons:

1. If you think of a date itself not as an extent in time but as a timestamp then 2012-03-20 is equal to 2012-03-20T00:00:00 which is the 0th second of 2012-03-20. 
2. In a lot of situations it's a lot more convenient to express it as half open intervals because you don't have to think about how many days are in a month. For instance if you run a bimonthly report then it's easier to run it for 2012-02-01-2012-02-15 and 2012-02-15-2012-03-01 rather than 2012-01-01-2012-02-14 and 2012-02-15-2012-02-29 (now you need to know the number of days of Feb 2012)

Back to the Axis/Scale issue, and after 3 years of thinking about the problem, I believe the best approach to cover all the cases is to support this metadata in as many file formats as possible:
- Front/Forward facing Axis
- Right Axis
- Up Axis
- Units per Metre

These four values represent the transformation from model space to _Physical Space_

To illustrate, I'll give two examples: let's say we export two human heads from two different design applications, each working with different axis and scale conventions.

We do know that a human head has a known scale, and we do know that the nose of the head must always point forward, regardless of the axis convention, so you could end with something like this:

From Software A:
- Front: -Z
- Right: +X
- Top: +Y
- Scale: 1

From Software B:
- Front: Y
- Right: -X
- Top: Z
- Scale: 0.01

By taking these 4 parameters, it could be possible to construct a matrix capable of transforming between model space and physical space, and even between different physical spaces.

Also, notice that the resulting transformation can have right or left winding. The winding can be known by checking the sign of the determinant of the matrix. If the origin transform has a different winding than the destination transform, (because they have opposite sign determinant), then we know after doing the transformation, we have to flip the faces.

Now, the problem lies in the fact that many formats miss this information. As far as I know, the only format that has all the metadata required is FBX. Problably Blender too. I think Collada has Up Axis and Scale, missing Front and Right (but I might be wrong)... for all others, maybe the convention could be inferred or assumed.

Thanks for finding the issue! This particular bug is over in s3transfer, so lets track it there: https://github.com/boto/s3transfer/issues/85

1. Yes, boto3 implements exponential backoff.  This request should be retried as shown [here](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/_retry.json#L114-L127).  In that file there's a `__defaults__` section, which shows we retry 5 times.  If you're seeing these errors it's because you're exceeding the 5 attempts we make. from pyramid.renderers import get_renderer

    master = get_renderer('templates/master.pt').implementation()
    event['master'] = master
```

I traced the issue in the OP, and it seems to me here's what's happening:

- `easy_install` is creating a temporary directory in which to extract the tarball.
- `sandbox` [invokes setup.py with `__file__` set to the setup script](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/v32.1.0/setuptools/sandbox.py#L244-L246)
- [Cython uses `__file__`](https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/0.24.1/setup.py#L98) to construct arguments to Extension.
- If the temporary directory was created as a unicode string, so also will be the arguments to Extension.

Presumably, this issue might also occur if distutils were invoked from a directory containing non-ascii characters. I'll test that theory.

Interesting. Btw, also worth mentioning the right way to test virtualenv would be assuming PATH doesn't contain any system-wide python folder, otherwise these types of bugs will be missed silently.

For instance, if i would have python3.6.2 in my PATH python3.dll would be reachable in the first place and pyqt5 would have been loaded correctly (ie: where python3.dll), i think is fair to assume a virtualenv won't rely on PATH containing any python paths.

Also, as you can see, the fix provides a manual list of python dlls instead of something more generic like copying the glob of `*.dll` or `python*.dll`, that way you can have a finer control over the copying process and being minimalistic :)

The basic issue is that the only way pykafka currently has to get a consumer or producer instance is to call a method on a `Topic` instance. Supporting multi-topic consumers and producers would require adding a method to `KafkaClient` like `get_consumer(topics)` that accepts a sequence of `Topic` instances. Beyond that, `SimpleConsumer` would have to hold a sequence of `Topic`s rather than a single one, and `consume()` might have to incorporate a `topics` parameter to control which topic is being consumed from. This in turn would require the consumer to store its queued messages with some way to retrieve them on a per-topic basis. The way the consumer holds its partitions would also have to be altered. I admit that this sounds like only slightly less problematic of a change than I'd anticipated. It's the kind of thing that I'd be able to work on over the course of a few Fridays. Of course, if anyone on this thread would like to take a crack at it, I'm very open to a substantive pull request addressing this issue.

Thinking about this again - we should maybe keep ``ez_setup.py`` for the foreseeable future. It turns out that if you want to run e.g. ``python setup.py test`` or ``python setup.py build_docs`` then you still need ``ez_setup.py`` to make sure setuptools is installed. I asked the setuptools folks and they said that the idea is that people should actually not use setup.py commands anymore in the long term: https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/1156 - of course a short term solution is to simply require setuptools to be installed before running ``setup.py``, but in that case we should make it explicit in the docs. This wouldn't be a problem for anyone installing astropy with conda or pip, but would be for anyone running commands in the repo (so mostly developers).

So the options for now are either keep the ``ez_setup.py`` file or require ``setuptools`` for people who want to use ``setup.py`` in some way.

Hey @krassif. Stripe's Python bindings can use different HTTP libraries. We recommend using `requests` if possible.

`requests` will automatically detect and use the `HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY` environment variables: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#proxies, so that's what I'd recommend.

If using the environment variables is not possible, then you'll have to modify the bindings themselves as we don't currently provide a way to set the proxy parameters programmatically. You'd need to modify [this call](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/master/stripe/http_client.py#L98-L103) to add the `proxies` parameter.

Hi Guido,

Thanks for catching this issue. I am trying to work though the refresh() code since I am having some trouble retracing my own work. Here are my notes on the issue:

* When call Workflow.refresh(), the immediate thing to do is to refresh the state of the given fw_id. This might mean that the fw_id is going from "RESERVED" to "RUNNING" (e.g. if a launch has started running) or a fw_id is going from "WAITING" to "READY" (if all parents have completed) or "RUNNING" to "COMPLETED" (if the launch completed), etc. So far, so good since we are localizing the update to the fw_id that was actually specified by the user.
* If the state of the fw_id changed in any way, we also add it to "updated_ids". The updated_ids are returned back to the calling function. For example, the LaunchPad uses the list of of updated_ids when calling workflow.refresh() to know which entries to update in the database (rather than updating the entire Workflow).
* Next, if we ended up going to a COMPLETED state for that fw_id, there is code to apply the FWAction (whether it is to defuse the children, pass information to the children, detour around children, add new workflows, etc). Note that by applying this action, we will also change the information on the children. So, we need to add those children to the list of updated_ids so that the calling function knows they have been changed and can take appropriate action (e.g., re-insert the changed children information into the database)
* Finally, we want to call "refresh" on the children of the current fw_id. The only reason I can think to do this is that if the current fw_id is moved to COMPLETE or FIZZLED from its previous state. Then, the children might need to be refreshed from "WAITING" to "READY" (in the case of FIZZLED, only when "_allow_fizzled_parents" is on). I cannot think of another reason to refresh the children of the given fw_id other than this situation. e.g., even if a fw_id moves from READY to RUNNING or DEFUSED, or WAITING to READY, I don't see a reason for refreshing children.

So here are the two things I don't understand:
1. Why - even before "defuse_workflow" existed - is it necessary to refresh children unless the new state of the given fw_id is COMPLETED/FIZZLED.
2. Why - even if a whole bunch of fw_ids are now DEFUSED due to defuse_workflow - it is necessary to refresh them (i.e., refreshing all updated_ids).

Please let me know if you have any insight into points 1 and 2 that I am missing. The only thing I can think of is that, currently, "apply_action" might change the state of some Firework objects to be "DEFUSED" but it does not update the variable Workflow.fw_states to sync those changes. The latter was introduced by some others to speed up the operation of the FireWorks code, but it requires that the Workflow.fw_states variable is always kept in sync with the state of each Firework object. The refresh() call helps maintain this sync, and that is why refreshing *everything* that changed might have seemed necessary. But it might be possible to simply make sure that the Workflow.fw_states variable is updated properly in the apply_action() method.

4267c0b819c6cdedcaa5d1ed64f16f271617ca80

Can you see if this solves your problem? Also please let me know if you foresee any issues or problms in my discussion as this is a major change in how refresh() works!

Hi, I have interest in this issue, and implemented AVX2 / SS4 runtime switching (https://github.com/grafi-tt/pillow-simd/commit/96f18c6292df46c47e1bc8a9aa382e9658ba16d5)

The usage is like:

```Python
import PIL
from PIL import Image
import timeit

IMG_FILE = "image-1920x1200.png"

print(PIL.get_available_builds())
print(PIL.get_build())

img = Image.open(IMG_FILE)
result = timeit.timeit('img.resize((960, 600), resample=Image.BICUBIC)', globals=globals(), number=1000)
print(result)

PIL.set_build("SSE4")
img = Image.open(IMG_FILE)
result = timeit.timeit('img.resize((960, 600), resample=Image.BICUBIC)', globals=globals(), number=1000)
print(result)

PIL.set_build("AVX2")
img = Image.open(IMG_FILE)
result = timeit.timeit('img.resize((960, 600), resample=Image.BICUBIC)', globals=globals(), number=1000)
print(result)

PIL.set_build("SSE4")
img = Image.open(IMG_FILE)
result = timeit.timeit('img.resize((960, 600), resample=Image.BICUBIC)', globals=globals(), number=1000)
print(result)
```

Following the strategy @socketpair and @homm mentioned, I made the core imaging module (`_imaging`) built twice with appending distinct suffixes to their names. Runtime CPU checking is done in a tiny C module which calls `cpuid` instruction using inline assembly. I believe `py-cpuinfo` is overkill, especially for a module with few dependencies.

Generic build (AVX2 or SSE4 not used) is not available yet, as making it requires back-porting Pillow's non-SIMD codes. Though I could manage to do this, but it is difficult as I'm new to Pillow and Pillow-SIMD. I think it is an obvious work for developers used to the codebase of Pillow-SIMD.

If someone is interested, feel free to fork and improve my implementation; or if preferred, I'm happy to send a PR.

The issue comes from the use of [database options](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/settings/#std:setting-OPTIONS) in my project settings and more specifically of the [timeout delay for SQLite](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/databases/#database-is-locked-errors).

In that case, the databases list returned is (note the second element):

```py
{'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3', 'NAME': 'C:\\Users\\$USER\\Documents\\GitHub\\Perso\\django_prj\\django_prj.sqlite'}
{'timeout': 30}
```

Furthermore, the use of `dict[key]` is not recommended to ensure access to a dict value (see [this SO conversation](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/11041405/why-dict-getkey-instead-of-dictkey)).

I managed this with a quick workaround:

```py
for database in list(settings.DATABASES.values()):
    if "ENGINE" in database.keys() and 'mysql' in database.get('ENGINE').lower():
        INDEX_SEARCH_NAMES = False
```

Is what you propose something along the lines of?

```python
#config.py
.... 
'filters': {'myfacet': ('invenio_records_rest.facets.term_filter', 'myfield')}
```

IMHO above just wraps a function call and adds complexity the facets code. The output of ``terms_filter('myfield')`` is a callable with takes a single argument ``values``. I'm fine with adding support for either providing 1) a callable or 2) an import string to a callable. However extending to also providing arbitrary arguments to the callable I don't think is a good idea.

As I understand it, the main argument is that you want a pure dictionary in the configuration. However, many other places in Invenio also does not support pure dictionary configuration, hence if we decide to go with pure dictionaries in Records-REST we should apply it consistently throughout Invenio.

I think what you are trying to do can also be achieved by some simple scripting the configuration:

```python
# config.py
def setup_filters(env_options):
   # ....
   return {k: terms_filter(v) for k, v in options}

# ...
'filters': setup_filters(os.env['MY_RANCH_FILTERS']),
```

Alternatively, there's also the possibility to customise the config loading via http://pythonhosted.org/invenio-config/usage.html. See dirty example at https://github.com/zenodo/zenodo/blob/master/zenodo/factory.py#L65-L73

Okay, I have added more extensive tests with non-basic Python types. The most extensive one is `test_prevents_bad_string_formatting_key` as it tests `str.format` in three cases :

1. accessing a basic Python type in a basic Python list: this is allowed
2. accessing a basic Python type in a non-basic PersistentList: this is *not* allowed, unless explicitly allowed by setting `__allow_access_to_unprotected_subobjects__` on the PersistentList.
3. accessing a non-basic persistent Item in a non-basic persistent Folder: the `__roles__` on the Item are checked, and whatever other checks AccessControl does.

Number two in that list might be okay to allow. But the point here is: `str.format` should use the same rules that `guarded_getitem` uses. And `guarded_getitem` does not allow access in the case of number 2. See [`ZopeGuards.py`](https://github.com/zopefoundation/AccessControl/blob/2.13.15/src/AccessControl/ZopeGuards.py#L84-L86): it only allows unchecked access if both the list and the item are simple types.

To summarise for clarity: the only thing this PR changes, is that using `str.format` or `unicode.format` in restricted Python code now explicitly calls `guarded_getitem`.

Thanks for the advice, that can be useful for large ZCatalogs.

I was wrong about memory usage in `plone.memoize`. The code uses `plone.memoize.ram`, which is based on `zope.ramcache`, which has a default size limit (1000 objects) and maintains the cache size.

Another concern is transaction isolation. `zope.ramcache` tells [explicitly](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.ramcache/blob/04185a3f16158ec60fbaef9279066d84dc7121cc/src/zope/ramcache/ram.py#L32) that it shares cached data between all threads. That opens a possibility for various races, e.g.:

- catalog index counter is persistent, so its change will not be visible in other threads until transaction is committed
- a thread can index new object, run a query and cache its result set
- a parallel thread can invoke the same query and receive cached result set that contains new entries, indexed in a first thread but not committed yet

That is purely theoretical, the best way to check is adding a test that works with two ZODB connections.

Two pieces to this:

Yes, we seriously considered Arrow (formerly Feather) as the main backend format for storing data on-disk. However, after careful consideration, we had to conclude that the format could not meet our needs:
* ~No support for string columns, only bytes~ (`Utf8` column type was added since then);
* No support for arrays with ≥2^31 elements, which means that datasets with over 2B rows cannot be stored contiguously. This limitation also applies to the total length of all items in an array-valued column, which means for example that a string column cannot have over 2GB of data in it.
* No support for in-place NA values (R-style). Without this we would not be able to memmap data from disk directly, and lose the ability to work with datasets that do not fit into RAM.

In terms of saving datatable Frames into or loading from feather files, this feature was included in our original roadmap (#290), and it still remains there.

Unfortunately, barman or node with nosync tag can become synchronous node not temporarily, but permanently until established sync replication stopped. Even when better candidate has already appeared. That's the issue.

I just realized this won't work in a heterogeneous cluster environment. Provided one or more replicas was promoted at a previous time and produced WAL traffic, and were later shut down, a lagging node could start and start a split timeline. Any initial startup **must** begin in recovery in an attempt to consume any distributed WAL files potentially produced by other intermediate primaries.

However, I will note that Patroni, as distributed, does not account for WAL archive broadcast necessary to handle this scenario. Documentation should strongly recommend Postgres 9.5 and above to make use of the 'always' setting for `archive_mode` to automate *some* of this process. An additional mechanism is necessary to ensure all unique WAL files reach all nodes.

Perhaps this should be retitled: "Patroni can not survive mixed-node WAL archive replay scenarios."

Well, that's not it being optional. That's deferring the handling of the fact that it is required. With a Swagger spec you know all the endpoints, and you can know in advance that the Operation does not resolve. As a dev (-opper) I'd rather know sooner than later.

As I said, a sensible default would be the method name. HTTP methods are perfectly good verbs to use as operation names.

Then you need to know which module to look in, which I figure can look like this:

```python
def detect_controller(self):

    operation_id = self.operation['operationId']

    # Since operationId should be a unique identifier, never combine it with x_router_controller
    # See https://github.com/apigee-127/swagger-tools/issues/221
    if operation_id:
        return operation_id

    x_router_controller = self.operation['x-swagger-router-controller']

    if x_router_controller:
        mod_name = x_router_controller
    else:
        mod_name = self.default_router_controller_name

    return mod_name + '.' + self.method
```

Where `default_router_controller_name` should be passed down from `connexxion.App`, defaulting to `import_name`.

    db.session.rollback()
    response = connexion.problem(
        status=HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
        title='Bad request',
        detail=error.message or error.__class__.__name__,
    )
    return response.flask_response_object()

```

Hi @MattiSG this is IMHO a good direction to move forward 😃. I've had experienced this issue myself.

> Country Template: pluralise first argument name in all formulas, using the pluralised name of the entity.
> Doc: pluralise first argument name in all formulas, using the pluralised name of the entity.
> Doc: document the recommendation to use the pluralised name of the entity as the first formula parameter.

I've passed quite a lot of time of my performance improvement efforts trying to understand the nature of the receiving arguments. Given the lack of native type check in `Python` (at least < 3.6), it can be a bit cumbersome.

I think naming should more or less reflect the _duck-typing_ of the argument. Whether it is a `list`, `tuple`, `set` or a `numpy.ndarray`, I think argument name should be pluralised. Even if argument is an empty collection or if it has just one element.

(Note: it goes beyond the current RFC, but arguments, if not optional, should respect _duck-typing_, i.e. not passing `None` where a `list` is expected).

> I am not so sure that pluralizing first argument will help a lot when the vector nature of the argument is made clearer and emphasized at the beginning of the training.

I see two other arguments for this:

1. It is easier IMHO to foster contribution if the fact that we're dealing with a vector is self-evident. We can only do a limited amount of training, for the rest we rely on the doc, the code and the tests.

2. It is way easier to refactor and to improve code when we know more or less the signature of functions and their returning type. If I can _see_ I'm dealing with vectors, I'll adapt my refactoring approach immediately.

I agree that the wording is not clear.

The idea is that any of the values for the ‘parameter’ can be specified as an integer or as a real number. If it is specified as an integer, the value is interpreted as a decipoint. If it is specified as a real number, it is interpreted as a real number. “15” and “1.5” are interpreted as indicating the same value.

The phrase "but a decimal point and following value is then required” is trying to make the point that number string cannot end with a decimal point: an additional digit must follow the decimal point. For example,  “1.0" is valid input, but “1.” is not.

- Read

This would require making the API reference less DRY, as we would need to explicitly name each method (to pull in its docstring) before the example.  In exchange, we would gain some benefits:

- We could explicitly reorder (or exclude) the method API reference entries.
- The Sphinx API source documents would become a logical place to inject other, too-verbose-for-the-docstring narrative.
- We would be able to use `testsetup` and `testcleanup` groups without trying to sync them between docstrings and the Sphinx source.

@jgeewax and @pcostell I am curious what you think of these strategies:

## Choice 1

- Store `_meanings` as a dictionary on `Entity`
- Set `_meanings` on object creation (when reading from datastore)
- Use the original never-updated `_meanings` from an `Entity` when serializing as a `pb` object (no matter what has happened in the meantime)

## Choice 2

- Store `_meanings` as a dictionary on `Entity`
- Set `_meanings` on creation (when reading from datastore)
- Over-write `__getitem__`, `__setitem__`, `__delitem__`, `update` and countless other `dict` methods to make sure that `_meanings` gets updated appropriately (this brings up a question, if we update a property, do we leave the meaning alone or check against the current type or raise an error if it gets changed or something else)

-----------

I am partial to **Choice 1** because re-implementing `dict` (in **Choice 2**) defeats the purpose of having `Entity` subclass `dict`. Maybe the presence of such a choice is just a sign we should bite the bullet and just implement `Entity` in an ORM-like fashion? (This would be a big change for our users, but we've always said "just wait, `ndb` is coming, which is also why we never went the ORM route.)

-----------

As a "modification" to **Choice 1**, we could also store something like

```python
_meanings = {
    'prop1': (DATETIME_MEANING, datetime.datetime),
    'prop2': (EMAIL_MEANING, str),
    ...
}
```

and then throw an error on serialization if

- Any of the properties have disappeared
- Any of the properties have changed type

-----------

Also as a modification to **Choice 1** (in the name of leaving `dict` alone), we could also turn off use of `meaning` by default and require users to have a flag to enable it. In the "turned off" case we could just throw an exception if they encounter data with a meaning and ask them to explicitly turn it on. In the explicit case, it would "alert" them to be careful and we could provide an API surface like

- `Entity.has_meaning('prop1')`
- `Entity.set_meaning('prop1', SOME_MEANING_ENUM.ENUM_VAL)`
- `Entity.get_meaning('prop1')`
- `Entity.clear_meaning('prop1')`
- `Entity.list_meanings()`

So, yes, `pathos.multiprocessing.Pool` uses `apply` and `apply_async`, while `pathos.pools.ProcessPool` uses `pipe` and `apipe`.  They are essentially the same... the code in `pools` is the newer interface.

The code you have just above (with `apipe` and `pipe`), when the pool only has one worker, it will only spawn one process to do all of the work.  Also note you are spawning a **process** not **threads**.  If you want threads, you want `pathos.pools.ThreadPool` (and/or `pathos.threading.Pool`)

And, I meant, this, which is simpler:
`results = [pr.profile('cumulative', pipe=ppool.apipe)(make_dict, str(i)) for i in (0,1)]`,
and it returns a list of asynchronous `ApplyResult` objects. The only issue with it, is as you pointed out, the Pool requires there be only one worker.

So, if you want more than one thread to do the work, yes, you need multiple pools to do what you seem to want to do.

> When you get an invalid dist, it's effectively impossible to know why...

the specific problem we saw that led to this ticket was a truncated read that was only exposed by checking Content-Length. when we repro'd with curl, we noticed it was returning an (unchecked) `CURLE_PARTIAL_FILE (18)` exit code.

so I think there's a clear precedent for checking this header as a basic means of size validation (and ultimately, that's why `Content-Length` exists in the HTTP spec) - it just happens to be unfortunate that it's not as simple as flipping on a flag in `requests` in particular. it seemed like the urllib3 guys were amenable to an upstream change to make that easier tho?

(fwiw, I hadn't meant to dissuade us from going this route with my comments, but was just pointing out some potential stumbling blocks.)

> In-addition to doing #2, it would be great if there were checksums in place on the server side, but I don't know whether such a standard exists for python dists.

the idea behind "findlinks" repos is that they're plain old dumb web servers that you scrape `A href` links from, so there's definitely no standard where server side checksums would fit in there. a true pypi style "index server" may tho, but that wouldn't benefit us currently. but: there is existing client-side checksum support for payload integrity checking, we just don't take advantage of it today.

> It prevents putting something broken in the cache, and allows you to indicate to the user which thing from which url was broken. So it solves the "opaque error" issue in the description, and prevents it from recurring when you retry.

yeah, I could see how that could be generally useful and it would solve the "opaque error" problem to a degree (it would tell you the source of the problem, but not the reason). perhaps the validation could be done inline as we're fetching via e.g. `StreamFilelike`?

this still doesnt solve for inline retries on a badly behaving server/network tho - we'd still end up with intermittent failures in CI for these cases. either way, the "detect a fetch vs content problem" + retry aspect still feels important to me for robustness.

Another option would involve only supporting pinning via the artifact cache. This would force us to implement artifact caching for the python pipeline (which we don't today) and only users who enable a remote artifact cache would benefit. Several problems here though:
1. The artifact cache fundamentally is transient. It save work when it can, but it can be blown away, LRU'd, etc. This would wipe out the pinned resolve.
2. A python binary that gained or lost platforms would invalidate a pinned resolve when it probably shouldn't. We'd likely instead want the versions to stay pinned as-is with just the platform-specific requirements added-removed to be added-removed from the resolve, but with the already established versions. Some handwave here, since the non-existence of a platform-specific probably affects version range solutions.

Hard to tell from an artificial example your exact goals, but both of these examples both work. I prefer the 2nd example but it doesn't meet your specific requirement of 2 different child class types.

**Example 1**
```
import related


@related.mutable()
class BaseChild(object):
    type = related.StringField()


@related.mutable()
class ChildA(BaseChild):
    field_a = related.IntegerField()


@related.mutable()
class ChildB(BaseChild):
    field_b = related.IntegerField()


@related.mutable()
class Parent(object):
    A = related.ChildField(ChildA)
    B = related.ChildField(ChildB)


@related.mutable()
class Root(object):
    conf = related.ChildField(Parent)


example = """
conf:
  A:
    type: a
    field_a: 1
  B:
    type: b
    field_b: 2
""".strip()

roundtrip = related.to_yaml(related.from_yaml(example, Root)).strip()
print(roundtrip)
assert roundtrip == example
```

**Example 2**
```
@related.mutable()
class Child(BaseChild):
    name = related.StringField()
    type = related.StringField()
    field_a = related.IntegerField(required=False)
    field_b = related.IntegerField(required=False)


@related.mutable()
class Parent(object):
    conf = related.MappingField(Child, "name")


example = """
conf:
  A:
    type: a
    field_a: 1
  B:
    type: b
    field_b: 2
""".strip()

roundtrip = related.to_yaml(related.from_yaml(example, Root)).strip()
print(roundtrip)
assert roundtrip == example
```

Hope that helps!
Ian

> Also note that ipwb stores its config information in the ipfs config.

Unless there is a good reason for this, I would call it a very bad choice. Once possibility of a danger is that it can be overwritten by IPFS on updates. Additionally, it might add something in the config file that IPFS daemon may not be happy about it.

I am not sure if I understood it completely, but in general I don't like the idea of letting the client to select the CDXJ files for reply. CDXJ is nothing but an index that can be created incrementally and the number of such files can be as little as one or as many as practically impossible for a human to deal with them n the client side. Merging CDXJ files is a trivial task and there is no magic involved in that. What would be a better idea in my opinion is toprovide an administrative interface to manage collections (namespaces) and associate one or more CDX files to each collection, then the users can select those named collections and not worry too much about the underlying details.

@yashu-seth I would like to suggest a few changes in the API to make it more consistent with the rest of pgmpy.

1. `ContinuousFactor` should accept `variables` as a parameter. In pgmpy, we have tried to keep factor/CPD separate from the model structure and for that factor needs to have the information of the variables on which it is defined.
2. I would suggest to have a directory structure like this:
```
├── factors
│   ├── discrete
│   │     ├── Factor.py
│   │     ├── CPD.py
│   │     ├── other
│   ├── continuous
│   │     ├── Discretize.py
│   │     ├── ContinuousFactor.py
│   │     ├── other
```
3. I don't think we need to have separate files for all the discretize methods. They can be put in the same file.
4. You need to add `get_discrete_values` as an abstract method in the base class because it needs to be defined if the user inherits the base class.
5. Does the individual discretize classes need access to the `ContinuousFactor` object ? We should keep it separate from factor since discretize is a property/function of distribution. So, we should associate discretize with just the pdf.

Lots of new stuff in this rebase/push. Highlights:

- New `drop` link_strategy as requested. I realized that this can also be used to dry-run through a difficult linking job to scan for oversize subnets, before attempting the actual computation.
- With the `diag` keyword argument enabled, linking code can attach arbitrary bits of diagnostic information to each particle, returned as extra columns in the result DataFrame. A tutorial for this feature is in the works.
 - The only sane way to add the diagnostic info in `link_df` was to make a copy of the input DataFrame. This could break some poorly-written user code. So I added the `copy_features` option to turn on copying without diagnostics.
- Some minor performance enhancements, validated by asv benchmarking. When adaptive search and link diagnostics are turned *off*, the performance penalty due to this PR is zero, within error.
- Particle ID numbering no longer depends on ordering of a `set`.

Obviously we have greatly expanded the scope of the original PR. But the diagnostics were motivated by adaptive search, and they mess with some of the same pieces of code, so I decided to take the lazy approach and then get feedback.

You are using very old version of conda.`conda update conda` will bring you to Conda 4.3.16. Can you try again after updating it?

Unfortunately, there's a technical reason we can't do this, which is that older versions of conda do not support circular dependencies (since pip also depends on python), and since conda itself depends on python, this makes it impossible to update older versions of conda if this change is made (we did make this change for a little bit a while back, and ran into this problem). 

The only solution I can think of is to package pip with python, but not make the conda package a dependency (possibly also making pip itself an empty package for backwards compatibility, or just leaving it be, since conda will ignore packages with duplicate files).
