> I still don't understand why the always_copy flag doesn't take precedence.

Before encountering this issue with you, I would have said the hard-link fall-back-to-copy case was just fine for this particular code path dealing with storing prefix-rewritten files in temporary locations.  Now I agree that `always_copy` should mean "always copy."

>  Is it correct that the create code is called from the PrefixReplaceLinkAction class in path_actions.py? 

Yes.

> And if so, isn't the functionality of the super call to override the link type with copy?

Yes, but that copy applies from to the operation that's going from the package cache to the temporary location.  What's problematic now, and needs to be changed per my first comment in this post, is

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/c54d490419d79d07a618a2c3ddc0729f20560246/conda/core/path_actions.py#L412

Should be changed to

    create_link(source_path, self.target_full_path, LinkType.copy)

> Does it matter that "prefix_placeholder" is missing although the arguments are given as positional arguments?

The `__init__` method on the parent class does not take `prefix_placeholder`.  That argument is specific to `PrefixReplaceLinkAction`.

You're probably is that you're trying to pipe the output of `conda install` into the output of `pip install`, and then conda isn't getting it's required `y` when it asks for confirmation on the install plan.

    conda install -c conda-forge line_profiler | pip install geopy memory_profiler

This should instead be two separate commands

    conda install -c conda-forge line_profiler
    pip install geopy memory_profiler

Hello,

at the case page in Scout there is a box:

![skarmavbild 2015-06-11 kl 07 37 07](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/405278/8100502/b59f9d0a-100c-11e5-86bb-c3caf9679384.png)

Here one can add and remove phenotypic terms in hpo format, these looks like **HP:0010704**.
[Phenomizer](http://compbio.charite.de/phenomizer/) is a tool for generating HPO terms based on text descriptions of phenotypes.  
So if I go to phenomizer and enter a phenotypic description, looks like this:

![skarmavbild 2015-06-11 kl 07 43 14](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/405278/8100538/9e9a4b36-100d-11e5-8ec3-4aa57b838ee3.png)

Then i copy the hpo term, **HP:002070** in this case, to the "Phenotype" box in scout and click "add", like:

![skarmavbild 2015-06-11 kl 07 46 04](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/405278/8100564/f747364a-100d-11e5-8634-27f6a9ea0c86.png)

Add a coouple of terms in this way, the more terms that are added the more accurate gene list will be generated by Scout:

![skarmavbild 2015-06-11 kl 07 51 55](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/405278/8100628/c4eef678-100e-11e5-80c6-65aab451f21f.png)

Each time a phenotypic term is added or removed the user generated gene list for this case is updated.

To filter the variants based on this gene list, go to variants, bring up the filter and click **HPO Generated Gene List** and then **Filter Variants**.

![skarmavbild 2015-06-11 kl 07 53 33](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/405278/8100669/4307ab86-100f-11e5-9138-980aafb28093.png)

You will now only see variants that resides in the genes of the user generated gene list.
The result will probably be better if you are looking at the research variants.

Another way of defining gene lists is to write hgnc id:s to a file on your computer. This file should have one hgnc id per row and look like

![skarmavbild 2015-06-11 kl 08 07 16](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/405278/8100838/edd3884a-1010-11e5-98eb-c876a9fb4e11.png)


You can now upload the file to scout by clicking "Bläddra" and then "Upload" in the filter (see picture above). Now you will only see variants that overlap these genes. In this way the users can define multiple gene lists locally and then reuse them in their analysies.

Hope this helps!

The SAM file specification is intended to be ASCII only (based on discussions with the spec maintainers), with the intent that a future version will support UTF-8 encoding of certain attributes.  Until this is changed, pysam is behaving correctly, because your file is invalid.  

While utf-8 is fairly safe to use in SAM files, some unicode code points will break BAM encoding rules.  So encoding work-arounds at the pysam level are not sufficient and substantial changes to htslib will be needed to properly allow utf-8 support.  I know this isn't the most helpful answer, but the best fix is to stick to ASCII until the spec and htslib are updated.

`match` query is different than `query_string` query - it doesn't parse the query and assign special meaning to quotes, it just uses the whole text as an input to the analyzer used. The standard analyzer will, in this case, produce two tokens - `d` and `86h`. My assumption is that you have many documents that have `d` as a token in that field.

It looks like `ban` is some sort of identifier - in that case you should be using a `term` query on the `.keyword` subfield which will do an exact match:
```
 client.search(index="m", doc_type="z", body={"query":{"term":{"ban.keyword":"D-86H"}}})
```

What you're probably seeing is the batching of calls.  Passing all arguments to commands will not work in in all situations (there is a command length limit!).  On unixlike systems, coreutils provides a utility [`xargs`](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/xargs.1.html) to compute how many arguments to pass to avoid this limit.   pre-commit implements a similar helper but is [extremely timid](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/51ac0e8bc0a1b61939283254c4bd5189c919f919/pre_commit/xargs.py#L8-L10) due to limits found on windows.

This limit can probably be increased on platforms where the limit is higher (if this can be detected?) and then you'll be much more likely to get one in a single batch.  I'd love a PR that does this :D

discord.py can request json like so (They basically made a class that creates their own ``request function`` that has an param named ``json``

```py
import aiohttp
import asyncio
import json

class HTTPClient:
    def __init__(self, loop, *args, *kwargs):
        self.loop = loop
        self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=self.loop, *args, **kwargs)

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def request(self, url, method, **kwargs):
        if 'json' in kwargs:
            headers['Content-Type'] = 'application/json'
            kwargs['data'] = json.dumps(kwargs.pop('json'), separators=(',', ':'), ensure_ascii=True)
        r = yield from self.session.request(method, url, **kwargs)
        yield from r.release()

```

As you can see json can be passed just fine into ``data=``.

If others end up here from a search, the relevant docs are at https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/client_advanced.html#disabling-content-type-validation-for-json-responses

> ## Disabling content type validation for JSON responses
> 
> The standard explicitly restricts JSON `Content-Type` HTTP header to `application/json` or any extended form, e.g. `application/vnd.custom-type+json`. Unfortunately, some servers send a wrong type, like `text/html`.
>
> This can be worked around in two ways:
>
> 1. Pass the expected type explicitly (in this case checking will be strict, without the extended form support, so `custom/xxx+type` won’t be accepted):
>  
> `await resp.json(content_type='custom/type')`.
>  
> 2. Disable the check entirely:
> 
> `await resp.json(content_type=None)`.

Hi! 

I presume the issue here is that the PyPI builds for aiohttp are generated via two different means - TravisCI for Linux/OS X and CircleCI for OS X, and that the latter doesn't run any tests so occurs almost instantly, whereas the former doesn't upload until 40 minutes later, after the tests have run.

It seems like shortening the test duration for Travis (eg by running the linux/OS X tests in parallel, rather than in series, and running on the faster fully-virtualised GCE infra) and also adding tests for CircleCI would at least bring the 2mins vs 40 mins divide closer. Failing that, perhaps the CircleCI publish step should just include a `sleep` 😆 

If the gap were more like <5 minutes, then it would seem reasonable for pyup to always just pause for a few minutes before opening the PR. (This would also have the added benefit of allowing time for the tagged GitHub commits to be pushed for people who use a manual workflow, ensuring the pyup generated changelog included the most recent release.)

It is deliberate, and it is a sort-of security measure. We don't want to expose your whole filesystem over HTTP. It's not a real security boundary, because someone with full access to the server can start a kernel and read files through that, but it means someone needs more than a simple HTTP GET to read files above the directory where you launched it.

I think it's a mistake for debian packages to unbundle npm dependencies like this. Each npm package that's part of a given application (e.g. jupyter-notebook, or any actually npm-based application) should include all of its own dependencies *in the package*, and not unbundle/link the npm dependencies as their own debian packages. That's not how npm packages work, and it's now how they should work when installed via other systems. While there may be some expansion of the build process for Debian's additional audit requirements, adding npm dependencies to a package should not result in requiring the creation of any additional debian packages.

A year ago or so I was on the brink of writing a dependency resolution module myself and I'm now happy that it went quickly over my head and I never came around to actually doing it. What that stint taught me tho is that file modification dates are a relatively poor proxy for like anything; on the one hand, there are file systems with a granularity of one entire second (OSX HPFS used to be among them), and on the other, a mere `touch`-like action can make a file look arbitrarily new anytime (not to speak of the vagaries that come with unpacking from archives or explicit setting of dates to the past or future). 

Hence, I've come to use file dates only as cheap excuse or last straw; the only real thing to me is file contents. I fully understand that Peru does and should resist the urge to manage in-situ dependency edits or do merges, with or without supervision. But this is not my point; my suggestion is to obtain a `sha1sum` from each file in the newly obtained (or cached) copy of the guest project, and then check each of those content hashes against those in the already installed copy of the guest. A correspondence of all hashes cannot possibly allow for any undetected change in the installed copy.

BTW I just tried and deleted a file from an installed guest copy, then did `peru reup`, and voila, I did get it back without even a notice; I gather this is the intended behavior. But then I did `touch` that same file, and `peru reup` ran without complaints. Then I changed one character (Peru complains) and changed it back (Peru is silent). Tried the same with a minimally updated guest version in the repo, same. Now I'm confused, because hours ago I did an `sah1sum` on each of the files in question, and they were source-identical, and yet Peru complained. That looked like file dates being to new, but the experiment I did as I'm writing this would indicate otherwise.

Irregardless of this particular bug: blockstack apps are writing across an unreliable network, (the internet!) that has no guaranteed quality of service and any number of things that can (and will!) break.  Using gaia storage isn't like writing to your database in a centralized app where you control the connection between your application server and the database. 

Reads and writes can fail for any number of reasons: because the network is down, the gaia hub is temporarily unreachable, because there are bugs in software somewhere, etc. Apps should be built to be able to handle and recover from read / write errors. For example, if there's an error, they could transparently retry the write.

In the future, we can consider moving this unreliable network handling logic into blockstack.js, but at the moment it's not there.

In this case, it appears the failure is from a bug that results from a particular combination of software. We *do* need to fix this. However, an app written to handle reading and writing from an unreliable media as described above shouldn't be affected by this bug. In other words, the best way to handle this particular bug in the next week or so is to make sure your app has logic to handle reads and/or writes that fail.

Having talked out-of-band with @kantai, I have a hunch that this might be related to the fact that the API server (`class BlockstackAPIEndpoint`) is derived from the `SocketServer.ThreadingMixin` class.  The specific bugs @kantai saw in the Chrome console looked like some HTTP requests were invalid because *the packet stream itself got cut off*.  I don't have a good explanation as to why this appears this way, but my gut feeling is that might be some sort of data race in the Mac OS `SockerServer` implementation.

I would be curious to know if this bug goes away when this parent class is removed (in `blockstack_client/rpc.py`).

I can reproduce when using a token.

When the `user` object is created from `network.get_authenticated_user()`, it sets the `User.name` from `network.username`, which is blank.

Previously this didn't matter: `AuthenticatedUser.get_name()` was fetching the username via [`user.getInfo`](https://www.last.fm/api/show/user.getInfo), but that started failing with "Operation failed - Most likely the backend service failed. Please try again" so I removed it in https://github.com/pylast/pylast/commit/000993c3289405c9d5a50c896c47795a52075608.

But this does look like a Last.fm bug; calling [`user.getInfo`](https://www.last.fm/api/show/user.getInfo) should be possible without the username:

> **_user_** (Optional) : The user to fetch info for. Defaults to the authenticated user.

---

One workaround is to pass the username when creating the `network`.

```python
>>> network = pylast.LastFMNetwork(
    api_key=LASTFM_API_KEY,
    api_secret=LASTFM_API_SECRET,
    token=TOKEN_GENERATED_BY_LASTFM,
    username=username,
)
>>> user = network.get_authenticated_user()
...
```

Then that is stored in `network.username`, and them in `User.name`, meaning the call to [`user.getInfo`](https://www.last.fm/api/show/user.getInfo) now has a parameter.

---

Is this workaround any use for you?

And please could you report the "backend failed" problem to Last.fm at https://getsatisfaction.com/lastfm/categories/lastfm_api?

I'm also running into this. I have a lot of flake8 plugins that implicitly enable a bunch of different codes, all of which get lost when I specify an explicit `select` in my config (unless I duplicate the list, but I do not really care much for that).

Looking at the code, it seems that `enabled-extensions` causes the plugin manager to enable these plugins: https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8/blob/52210149474a876c06922ae2c296445af5bdb108/src/flake8/plugins/manager.py#L214-L215

Enabling a plugin, causes its "name" (which is really a prefix for codes) to be added to the extended select list (which causes the code to be implicitly selected). https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8/blob/52210149474a876c06922ae2c296445af5bdb108/src/flake8/plugins/manager.py#L188-L191

The "name" for bugbear is "B": https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8-bugbear/blob/6bf40eafe99bb569296c460b691a6c0bebd27ea5/setup.py#L60

AFAICS, the bugbear plugin is enabled by default, so `enable_extensions` is essentially a no-op.

One way to fix this would be to let bugbear specify multiple entrypoints: One for B0 and one for B3, both of which are enabled by default (like now), and one for B9 which is disabled by default: https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8/blob/52210149474a876c06922ae2c296445af5bdb108/src/flake8/plugins/manager.py#L516-L517

AFAICS only one of these three plugins actually has to do work, the others can be empty and just serve to prefill the list of included codes. If you do this, you can use `enable_extensions` to enable the `B9` plugin, without having to specify `select`.

However, I'm not exactly sure that this is really reliable, since I'm not sure if this empty plugin approach is really future-proof (or that it works at all...).
I guess this is really a problem in flake8, though. Ideally, it would process:
 - Default select rules
 - Default ignore rules (empty)

The interface for parallel evaluations is exposed in the `cma` package via the `cma.CMAEvolutionStrategy.ask` method, which returns a list of solutions to be evaluated. This leaves us with the desire for a wrapper making parallel calls to the objective function with these solutions. That is, we want to parallelise the line
```python
f_values = [f(x) for x in X]
```
where `X` is a list of `numpy` arrays and `f` is the objective function (considered to be CPU-expensive).

I haven't seen something like a three-liner to do this in Python yet and currently cannot provide any ready-to-go code doing so. If someone can help out that would be great. I am looking forward to any contribution to this end!

@truunk If you're already using System.Configuration for library/app settings you'll be able to use System.Configuration.ConfigurationManager to continue to do so cross framework (we needed to name the  assembly differently from NetFX to allow running against it, 99% of System.Configuration is there).

As you've noted, we haven't provided guidance on how to deploy settings in Core- we don't use System.Configuration ourselves. While we didn't have the library in CoreFX until now, we're unlikely to use it. The key reasons for that is that System.Configuration brings in a lot of dependencies (heavy) and those dependencies need the ability to read settings (notably XML).

While we don't use them internally, config files are a pattern that is fairly well understood and can continue to be used. While it isn't part of the official .NET Standard 2.0 it is fully cross plat outside of the built-in encrypted Protected Sections (which is a bit obscure).

We're definitely interested in your continued thoughts on the matter and will try to come up with some clear guidance.

Here's what happened in my understanding:

1. **T1** creates the listening socket **SOCK3**
2. **T1** creates and starts thread **T2**
3. **T2** calling `select` on the **SOCK3**, and being blocked by it
4. **T1** creates the client socket **SOCK4**, and tried to connect to the listening port
5. **T1** closes both sockets **SOCK3** and **SOCK4**, but **T2** is still blocking by `select` on **SOCK3**
6. **T1** joins **T2**, and results in deadlock

Note:
* **T1** is the main thread
* **T2** is the `serve_forever2()` thread
* **SOCK3** is the listening socket which has fd=3 in the strace log
* **SOCK4** is the client socket tried to connect to listening socket, it has fd=4 in the strace log

I would guess, the root cause of the issue is the blocked `select` function call will not return if the socket has closed by another thread.

For concrete reference of others later, the way someone at my client managed to break this was "[`pip install awscli`](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/awscli-install-linux.html#awscli-install-linux-awscli)", which dragged in (through some dependencies) a newer version of `python-dateutil` than the one that had been installed through the Ubuntu system packages (ie, `apt-get install python-dateutil` -- currently 2.4.2).  Since two package managers are then involved... they each do their own thing, and anything without an isolated environment (`virtualenv` / `docker` / ...) will get the confusing mix of both.  (FTR, holding the package in Ubuntu is pretty trivial, eg, "`dpkg hold python-dateutil`" -- but obviously doesn't affect what `pip` will do separately...)

AFAIK `setup.py` / `requirements.txt` will prevent *installs* if the requirements don't match; but won't prevent against some later upgrade of the "system wide" packages that then mean that those "negative requirements" (ie, "no later than...") aren't violated later by other upgrades.  In an automated CI/CD environment where you always redepoly everything into a fresh instance maybe you find out when the install fails, rather than due to data corruption.  But this client isn't all the way there yet :-(   (Nor, obviously, does everything run in its own isolated environment :-( )

FTR, I do agree that with hindsight the `dayfirst` / `yearfirst` API was an unfortunate choice, and insufficiently expressive for what real world uses required.  Hence my suggestion of `expected_formats` with a list as a more expressive way to indicate what to do when the date is ambiguous.

But I'm also concerned that this has, effectively, been a 2 year long regression in the way that "European and ISO8601" date handling worked, biting an increasing number of people as they get upgraded for other reasons.   With the only workarounds being (a) try harder to avoid upgrading, (b) stop using the library (eg, use `strptime()` directly) or (c) wait for some eventual later version with a better API.   Given that apparently the regression was caused by a change to support a date format (Y-D-M) that in practice isn't even used that doesn't seem like the ideal tradeoff IMHO.  (But maybe after two years there are people relying on the "new" behaviour too.)

My client chose to standardised on ISO8601 (as of today definitively; most were already), set `dayfirst=False` / `yearfirst=True`, and we're fixing up the couple of days of corrupted dates.   Of note for anyone finding this later, with `dayfirst=True` parsing ISO8601 dates will invert `day`/`month` *if and only if* the third part (`day` in ISO8601) is between 1 and 12 inclusive; otherwise it won't.  So the cleanup also needs to be aware that only some dates end up affected.

I would recommend not using `pytz` for this (and in fact, I would recommend not using it at all).

You can see [this not yet released blog post](https://blog.ganssle.io/drafts/pytz-fastest-footgun.html) where I go into detail about the differences between `pytz` and `dateutil`'s timezone models. I also cover it in [this talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l4UCKCo9FWY), if you're more of a video person.

The difference here is that `pytz` eagerly calculates offsets when you call `localize` on a given `datetime`, whereas the Python model intends these offsets to be calculated lazily on demand.

This is a duplicate of #102 and #88. The strategies you can use here are:

1. Switch to using `dateutil.tz`, in which case it will more or less just work
2. Strip off the time zone before generating the datetimes and then call `the_timezone.localize` on each `datetime` returned by `rrule`
3. Call `dt.tzinfo.normalize(dt)` on the results of all your `rrule` outputs.

As I mentioned above, I recommend the first option.

Some thoughts:

* I'm going to insist on consistent naming style for Python variables / fields
* I'd like to [avoid subclassing](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3MNVP9-hglc) if we can. One way we could do this is to rely on attrs to inspect fields:

    ```python
    def to_json_data(obj):
      return attr.asdict(obj)
    ```

   If we are worried about objects defining attributes that aren't part of the Grafana JSON schema (and we should be), then we can filter based on some metadata (see below). Another way is to have a class decorator, but I think that's unnecessary.
* I'm uncertain about automating this away:
  * PRO: less duplication, thus less scope for silly errors
  * CON: less explicit, which increases scope for difficult-to-understand errors
  * Do you have any more pros & cons?
* attrs allows attributes to [specify a `metadata` field](https://attrs.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html#attr.ib), perhaps we can use this to store the Grafana field mapping, e.g.

    ```python
    @attr.s
    class Graph(object):
      dataSource = attr.ib(metadata={'grafanaName': 'datasource'})
    ```
* We could even wrap this in a helper, `grafanaField`, so we can explicitly mark attributes as being exported

What do you think?

Doh!  What's a factor of 10 among friends... ;)

Anyway, the technique of blocking until the first char arrives (with normal timeout in effect), then "reading like heck" with a non-blocking read a short sleep until no new chars arrive appears to be very reliable.  And it has a good chance of working cross-platform (I'll know in a day or two.)

For posterity, here's what I've implemented, but I'd be delighted to learn how I can improve it by taking better advantage of pyserial's API:

    # Normally, you'd want to set up a separate thread for asynchronous reads,
    # but given the nature of master / slave responses, the foreground task
    # can't do much besides wait for a reply from the instrument, so we keep
    # it simple and single-threaded.
    #
    def read_response(self):
        '''
        Read Modbus response, where end-of-packet is indicated by a 
        gap in received bytes.
        '''
        response_packet = bytearray()
        instrument_timeout_at = time.perf_counter() + self._timeout
        in_packet = False

        # Step 1: Try to read one byte (synchronously).  If it times out, that
        # just means the instrument isn't responding.  If we get a byte, we can
        # enter the "fast" read cycle.
        self._serial.timeout = self._timeout
        data = self._serial.read(1)
        if len(data) == 0:
            return response_packet
        response_packet.extend(data)

        # Step 2: got the first byte.  Read the rest of the packet using async
        # reads with short sleeps to detect any gap (signifying end-of-packet).
        self._serial.timeout = 0
        while (True):
            # perform async reads
            data = self._serial.read(256)

            if len(data) > 0:
                # one or more bytes have arrived
                response_packet.extend(data)
                in_packet = True
                time.sleep(self._inter_packet_timeout)  # short sleep

            elif len(response_packet) > 256:
                # interface is spewing data.  make it stop!  :)
                break
            
            else:
                # actively collecting bytes, but none arrived since last tic
                break

        return response_packet
            
        if self._debugging: self.debug_print(debug_str)
        return response_packet

You could train your model offline using your full set of historical data, and afterwards update your model using the `LightFM.fit_partial` with the new data during production.

In this case your model will continue grow if you add new users/items that were not included in the initial `LightFM.fix`.

Does this example help?
```
from lightfm import LightFM
from lightfm.data import Dataset

# Make some fake data.
def fake_data(n):
    users = np.random.choice([0., 1., 2.], (n, 1))
    items = np.random.choice([0., 1., 2.], (n, 1))
    weight = np.random.rand(n,1)
    return np.concatenate((users, items, weight), axis=1)

train_data = fake_data(10)
new_data = fake_data(3)

# Use Dataset to prep your interactions and weights.
dataset = Dataset()

dataset.fit(users=np.unique(train_data[:, 0]), items=np.unique(train_data[:, 1]))
train_interactions, train_weights = dataset.build_interactions((i[0], i[1], i[2]) for i in train_data)

dataset.fit_partial(users=np.unique(new_data[:, 0]), items=np.unique(new_data[:, 1]))
new_interactions, new_weights = dataset.build_interactions((i[0], i[1], i[2]) for i in new_data)

# Fit the model using your full set of historic data.
solver = LightFM()
solver.fit(interactions=train_interactions, sample_weight=train_weights)

# In production update your old model with new data.
solver.fit_partial(interactions=new_interactions, sample_weight=new_weights)
```

The filter is (mostly) inherited from the matlab code, which apparently chose 87 degrees to match a FORTRAN implementation of the algorithm:

https://github.com/sandialabs/MATLAB_PV_LIB/blob/master/pvl_disc.m#L134

```
DNI(Z>87 | GHI<1 | DNI<0) = 0; % 87 degrees chosen to simulate the FORTRAN code in use by SRRL (from Perez)
```

I say "mostly" because we changed the ghi filter condition from 1 to 0 at some point, which was probably a mistake. The filter should be ``ghi|dni <= 0``, which for floating point numbers, means ``ghi|dni < 0 + a small number``. Maybe 1 is too big, but it shouldn't be 0.

I'm guessing that we can make a more permissive filter without needing to add an optional argument.

Is the zenith filter actually necessary if we already have the ghi and dni filters? It's not clear to me how the algorithm would behave without it. Maybe filtering on some other quantity (e.g. dni = 0 if Kn > 1?) would be preferable. 

The zenith filter is probably preventing nans in the airmass calculation, but you could handle that differently.

@wintersteiger 
> @cheshire: No need to be sorry, good to see people voice their opinion.
> 
> I thought the proposal is to turn relative imports into imports that are relative to src/, so that the build system doesn't have to figure out the sub-directories (which is already implemented).

The proposal is essentially that but the motivation is not only to simplify the build systems, it gives

* Greater clarity. A reader knows instantly which directory a header file lives in(and therefore which component it belongs to).

* Prevents header file naming conflicts. If two components had header files with the same name in the current system this would be pretty disastrous because it is not obvious which header file would be chosen. It's not a problem today but I very much think it's a good idea to set things up so it isn't so easy to "shoot yourself in the foot".

The "sub-libraries" things you were talking about is **not** my goal and I'm not sure why you brought that up. Sure the change I'm proposing in this issue could be a (very small) stepping stone to enabling "sub-libraries" but that's not what proposing. I'd rather hear your reasons for rejecting what I'm **actually proposing** than reasons to reject something I never proposed.

Maybe I've misunderstood what you mean by "sub-libraries" here but the change I'm proposing does not require any re-architecting the existing libraries. This proposal only aims to change the way ``#include ""``s are written in Z3's code, nothing else.

If supported it would foremost only be syntactic sugar for nested ifs, so it would be more of a way to make inputs convenient. It might be possible to use this to optimize how function definitions are matched, but I am not sure how far we can support this.

@AndyXan : @subwire and I have done some intensive debugging on this issue in the past few days. Our suspicion so far is that the way Python GC works triggered a race condition between `Z3_inc_xxx_ref()` and `Z3_dec_xxx_ref()`.

The PythonZ3 objects we use in angr, including the Z3 solver, the Z3 ASTs, the models, etc., all have corresponding C++ objects in Z3. Internally, Z3 performs a reference couting on those C++ objects, and will release/free those objects as soon as their reference counts get down to 0. In angr, we do not have to manually call the `inc_ref` or `dec_ref` APIs, since the Z3 Python binding does this job for us nicely: Any time a PythonZ3 object is created (like, a `z3.Solver` object), it will call Z3's interface to increment the reference counter, and when this object is deleted by Python's garbage collection, its `__del__()` method will be called, where it decrements the reference counter.

It is all fine if this reference counting only happens within the same thread. Unfortunately, when you manually create a thread in Python and uses Z3 from that thread, there are actually two Python threads: the main thread, and the manually created thread. It seems to me that Python's GC only happens inside the main thread, while all Z3 object creation now happens in your new thread. This causes a race condition, impacting the internal reference counting that Z3 does. As a result, you may see double-frees (when a reference counter goes down to 0 prematurely) or memory leaks (when a reference counter never goes down to 0).

Note that this is still our suspicion so far. We will verify our theory, and come up with fixes to address this issue. Thanks again for reporting!

Apparently an error occurred: `<PathGroup with 120 errored>` . It's probably a bug in either angr or your code. You can retry the path and see what the error was: `pg.errored[0].retry()`. It should print out the exception. Post the exception here and we'll see where to go from there.

The problem is `num_inst=1` and the instruction at the address being a branch instruction in MIPS. VEX requires the branch instruction and the jump slot be in the same block, so `num_inst` breaks that.

I debugged it, and found out the issue: `SimEngineVEX` will decode as many bytes as possible without considering section boundaries. It decodes a basic block starting from `0x4b8fe2` until `0x4b9001`. However, while `0x4b8fe2` is in `.code` section, `.data` section begins from `0x4b9000`.

`CFGFast` takes section boundaries into consideration, so it correctly terminates the basic block `0x4b8fe2` before reaching address `0x4b9000`.

Hence blocks of two different sizes are created (one is 31 bytes, the other is 30 bytes). The solution is to pass in a `size` to `SimEngineVEX.lift()` from angr - in this case specifically, from all places in `function_manager.py` where `self._kb._project.factory.snippet()` is called. 		time.sleep(.5)

	while not vehicle.armed:
		time.sleep(1)#Waiting for armed
	#Take Off
	thrust = 0.7
	while True:
		current_altitude = vehicle.location.global_relative_frame.alt
		if current_altitude >= Target*0.95:
			break
		elif current_altitude >= Target*0.6:
			thrust = 0.6
		set_attitude(thrust = thrust)
		time.sleep(0.2)

	def set_attitude(roll_angle = 0.0, pitch_angle = 0.0, yaw_rate = 0.0, thrust = 0.5, duration = 0):

	#Duration is seconds to do this for

	msg = vehicle.message_factory.set_attitude_target_encode(
		0,
		0,                                         #target system
		0,                                         #target component
		0b00000000,                                #type mask: bit 1 is LSB
		to_quaternion(roll_angle, pitch_angle),    #q
		0,                                         #body roll rate in radian
		0,                                         #body pitch rate in radian
		math.radians(yaw_rate),                    #body yaw rate in radian
		thrust)                                    #thrust

	vehicle.send_mavlink(msg)

	if duration != 0:
        	# Divide the duration into the frational and integer parts
        	modf = math.modf(duration)
        	
        	# Sleep for the fractional part
        	time.sleep(modf[0])
        	
        	# Send command to vehicle on 1 Hz cycle
        	for x in range(0,int(modf[1])):
        	    time.sleep(1)
        	    vehicle.send_mavlink(msg)

	def to_quaternion(roll = 0.0, pitch = 0.0, yaw = 0.0):
		"""
		Convert degrees to quaternions
		"""
		t0 = math.cos(math.radians(yaw * 0.5))
		t1 = math.sin(math.radians(yaw * 0.5))
		t2 = math.cos(math.radians(roll * 0.5))
		t3 = math.sin(math.radians(roll * 0.5))
		t4 = math.cos(math.radians(pitch * 0.5))
		t5 = math.sin(math.radians(pitch * 0.5))
    
		w = t0 * t2 * t4 + t1 * t3 * t5
		x = t0 * t3 * t4 - t1 * t2 * t5
		y = t0 * t2 * t5 + t1 * t3 * t4
		z = t1 * t2 * t4 - t0 * t3 * t5
    
		return [w, x, y, z]


	arm_and_takeoff(flightalt)

`-o` is implemented by sending the spark dataframe as JSON and then constructing the JSON into a pandas dataframe locally and putting that dataframe in a variable. We could potentially get the JSON representation of a local pandas dataframe, put it into some spark code that could transform the JSON representation into a spark dataframe, and put that into a variable. It's doable, there's just concerns around escaping the JSON right, handling multiple encodings, limiting the amount of data you can send, letting the user configure that, supporting the different versions of spark, and handling all of these for the multiple languages we support, both in the kernels and in the magic-based ipython kernel scenarios.

Sparkmagic is written in such a way that it's very easy to implement once and use that code in all kernels. If you are comfortable with spark and reading python code, you could try to tackle it, and I could support along the way.

```python

babel = Babel(app)

@app.route('/')
def hello_world():
    return gettext("Hello World")

app.run()
```

ISO importer used to give a vague error message when one or more ISOs in a repository could not be downloaded. So a good test for this would be to:

1. create an ISO repository in Pulp. 
2. Upload to the repository some files that will be used as fixtures in  the test.
3. Publish the ISO repository. 
4. Add the content of the published repository to github as a fixture for the test.
5. Remove one of the files (but not all files).
6. Use the new fixture repository (with a missing content unit) to test. 
7. Write a test that syncs this repository and make sure that the error message reported by the API says that the failure occurred due to a 404 for that particular file that is present in PULP_MANIFEST but not available to download.

1. You can combine multiple score addresses to use in a reward function in a lua script. See Sonic for an example of a reward function that relies on a lua script: https://github.com/openai/retro/blob/master/retro/data/stable/SonicTheHedgehog-Genesis/script.lua

2. I'm not super familiar with the Retro UI tool but I think it works like this. The bottom right part is a search tool and the top right is a watch tool. In the bottom right you search for variables that meet your criteria. Enter any name and then search by a criteria (ie it defaults to 'Is 0'). Hit search and then everything with value 0 appears (or whatever criteria you choose). Then you can click on the name in the Name/Found section. A bunch of different addresses that meet your criteria will appear in the Value/Address/Type section. Click on one of those addresses and then the 'Make Variable' button to make it into a variable. You can then watch that variable's information as the game plays out. Alternatively, you can make a variable to watch by inputting a name, address, and type in the 3 boxes above 'Scenario information.' In either section you can see the type.

[PyHook](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyHook) is not strictly necessary.

As I understand it, the main reason for using *PyHook* at all is to be able to hook into some of the hook types that require the callback function to reside in a DLL. As is described [here](http://stackoverflow.com/a/7460728), this is not necessary when using low-level hooks. The rewrite would thus focus on using the correct hooks and `ctypes.windll.user32.SetWindowsHookExW`. An example of such a hook can be found [here](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pynput/blob/master/lib/pynput/_util/win32.py).

All the methods and attributes prefixed by '_' are meant to be "protected", and overridable by subclasses. These methods are not meant to be *public* API, but I don't think that's what you actually want ?

> 1) Handling of responses getting routed to the wrong originator (as being discussed by @jwhui and @hubertmis)?

Note that this PR affects ***default*** source address selection.  Client code can (and should) specify a unicast address as its source address when responses must be routed back to the originator.

> 2) Security implications of overloading the source field (source no longer being a unicast address)?  I think there is little risk of a "duplicate nonce" issue because two nodes using same anycast will use different extended MAC64 in nonce, but does the receiving node need extra logic to run through all possible MAC64 peers to decrypt a message for a given anycast?

IPv6 source addresses have no relationship with AES Nonces used by the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC.  MLE does not use anycast addresses, so this should also not apply to AES Nonces used by MLE.

Thank you for your comment. I understand the name could be offensive for some people and I am really sorry for that. What I am about to say it's just context/history, I am not trying to defend the name or anything.

The project started as an internal tool long ago, as you can imagine, being an internal project the name never got a second thought and napalm was mostly a joke due to the frustrations we had with working with several vendors. After talking with several people in the community we realized there was common ground to work together so this internal tool was shared on github in a private repo so we could start collaborating. Before we realized, the tool had grown quite a bit and there was a bunch of people from different organizations working on it so we just decided to make the repo public and start sharing it with the community. Up to this point, nobody had really cared about the name, we were just fixing a problem without caring about what seemed like an non-important detail at the time, so the name just carried on. A couple of years later the project is quite popular and I understand it doesn't have the best name, believe me if I tell you that if I had known 3 years ago the project was going to grow so much I would have picked a more neutral name.

> I do understand if this seems like runaway political correctness and I expect this issue will be rejected

Regardless of the reason I think your points are still valid and it's not going to be rejected because we don't agree with you, I have considered myself this topic a couple of times, but changing the name of a project is a titanic effort that we probably can't afford to do ourselves (update docs, references in code, variables, classes, readmes, domain names, etc). This might sound as an excuse but this is an opensource project lead by volunteers with no political affiliations, no backing from any company or foundation and no funding so a task like that is hard to execute because it's a task that has to be done during our free time at the expense of bugfixes and new features. As long as the two experiments don't need to be aware of each other though this is definitely supported simply by using the same `env` and `win` inputs. As long as each plot uses a unique name they will be shown as separate lines on the same plot. 
Two caveats
1) you'll need to use the `legend` opt and give it a unique value for experiment 1 and experiment 2.
2) Only the first experiment to create the window should use `viz.line` without using the `update` parameter. All subsequent calls should update the existing one. This can be implemented using `viz.win_exists` as such:
```
# Exp 1
if not viz.win_exists('expPlot'):
    viz.line(X=xData, Y=yData, win='expPlot', opts=dict(legend=['Experiment 1']))
else:
    viz.line(X=xData, Y=yData, win='expPlot', name='Experiment 1', update=True)
    

# Exp 2
if not viz.win_exists('expPlot'):
    viz.line(X=xData, Y=yData, win='expPlot', opts=dict(legend=['Experiment 2']))
else:
    viz.line(X=xData, Y=yData, win='expPlot', name='Experiment 2', update=True)
```
If you also want to update these over time by appending rather than replacing a line, you can use the `update = 'append'` feature to append new data to the plot for a given line:
```
viz.line(
    X=newXData, 
    Y=newYVals, 
    win='expPlot, 
    name='Experiment 2',
    update='append'
)
```

Late to the party, but here are my two cents on dask-glm in particular: if people are gravitating towards dask-ml for fitting linear models, then I'd vote to merge dask-glm into dask-ml.  The original armchair intent of dask-glm was to better match optimization routines to (linear) model usage / intent, but I don't think there was as much appetite for that (or maybe I didn't socialize that well enough).  This would also concentrate the sparse activity on dask-glm into dask-ml which might lead to better cross-talk and interesting usage.  Clearly this would also increase the maintenance burden on dask-ml though, so that's something to consider.  

I think the two "features" of dask-glm that I'd want to persist are: easy / transparent ability to fit unregularized models, and ability to handcraft Regularizers without having to implement new optimization routines, but that's just me.

> It needs its own type_caster which uses ensure (as opposed to a simple check like the other pytypes). It will need to stay this way.

Agreed.

> `py::isinstance<array_t<T>>()` is not supported. This can probably be resolved, but there is no quick solution in the numpy C API, so a something custom would need to be made.

Do we want it to be supported? Note that there's also the flags aka requirements. I'm not sure there's an existing numpy function that checks exactly what we need, so we can check (1) `py::isinstance<array>`, (2) check that dtypes are equivalent, (3) check that flags are satisfied. @jagerman I don't think there's anything that can be detected. Disabling RTTI isn't part of the C++ standard so the `typeid()` call becomes undefined behavior -- no way around that. The C++ interface for the underlying C library is tricking the C++ compiler into working with the type, but the C library doesn't supply the data needed at runtime.

Re: your comment above, I agree, we can add a bunch of static asserts (like, if you specify c-style/f-style, you can't provide strides anymore).

Btw: should the same logic apply to contiguity flags vs. being able to access the `.data()`? E.g. see this issue - https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/issues/538#issuecomment-263884464 -- does it make sense to expose the data pointer if the memory storage is not known to be contiguous, would you be able to think of an example?

Re: copying, instead of using Eigen to copy out of numpy, we could add something to tell numpy to copy into a destination, which would avoid a copy in some cases, and avoid the need for the special case for negative strides.
I think it would also be nice to provide a convenient way to mark a wrapped class as unhashable, but I'm not sure what a good syntax for that would be.

The `method_whitelist` argument of `urllib3.Retry()` allows restricting the resending of a request upon timeout during reading of a response to certain HTTP methods, i.e. we could restrict it to just GET.

However, I did not see a single case yet where the HMC did not respond to a GET (or any other method) at some point. So the only reason to have a read timeout and read retry configured would be to have an additional safety net that guarantees that the zhmcclient layer returns to its caller at some point.

I'll post a PR that re-enables read timeout and read retry for just GET, with some sensible default values.

**Updated instructions.**

Start Redis

```
rm dump.rdb; ./python/ray/core/src/common/thirdparty/redis/src/redis-server --loadmodule python/ray/core/src/common/redis_module/libray_redis_module.so
```

Start the global scheduler (and pass in the Redis address)

```
./python/ray/core/src/global_scheduler/global_scheduler -r 127.0.0.1:6379
```

Start the plasma store

```
./python/ray/core/src/plasma/plasma_store -s /tmp/s1 -m 1000000000
```

Start the plasma manager

```
./python/ray/core/src/plasma/plasma_manager -s /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379 -h 127.0.0.1 -p 23894
```

Start the local scheduler

```
./python/ray/core/src/local_scheduler/local_scheduler -s /tmp/sched1 -p /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379 -a 127.0.0.1:23894 -h 127.0.0.1
```

Start a worker (or run this multiple times to start multiple workers).

```
python python/ray/workers/default_worker.py --redis-address 127.0.0.1:6379 --object-store-name /tmp/s1 --object-store-manager-name /tmp/m1 --local-scheduler-name /tmp/sched1 --node-ip-address 127.0.0.1
```

Connect a driver (run this in a Python interpreter).

```python
import ray

address_info = {
  "node_ip_address": "127.0.0.1",
  "redis_address": "127.0.0.1:6379",
  "store_socket_name": "/tmp/s1",
  "manager_socket_name": "/tmp/m1",
  "local_scheduler_socket_name": "/tmp/sched1"}

ray.connect(address_info, mode=ray.SCRIPT_MODE)
```

Some other useful things:
- Start a redis client with `redis-cli` and run `monitor` to monitor all of the commands going through the redis server.

> could you maybe change it so the plugin considers all input urls on windows as having " " around them?

Hi, @elrik75.

I've done some research on `asyncio` for progress notification. Also I moved progress-logic away from `execute` method to `execute_with_progress`.
Examples of futher usage are below:

Python 2.7 (no asyncio):
```python
from datetime import datetime
from clickhouse_driver.client import Client

c = Client('localhost')

# Query is sent synchronously.
progress = c.execute_with_progress('LONG AND COMPLICATED QUERY')
timeout = 60
started_at = datetime.now()

# Actual synchronous packets reading starts here. 
for p in progress:
    print(p)  # p = (num_rows, total_rows)
    now = datetime.now()
    if (now - started_at).total_seconds() > timeout:
        print("Too long, can't wait... Cancelling")
        c.cancel()
        break
else:
    rv = progress.get_result()
```

`execute_with_progress` + `get_result` = `execute`
```python
from clickhouse_driver.client import Client

c = Client('localhost')

progress = c.execute_with_progress('LONG AND COMPLICATED QUERY')
rv = progress.get_result()
```

Python 3.5+ (asyncio):
```python
from datetime import datetime
import asyncio

async def go_progress():
    client = AsyncClient('localhost')
    
    # Query is sent asynchronously. 
    progress = await client.execute_with_progress('LONG AND COMPLICATED QUERY')
    timeout = 60
    started_at = datetime.now()

    # Each progress packet is read asynchronously. 
    async for p in progress:
        print(p)  # p = (num_rows, total_rows)
        now = datetime.now()
        if (now - started_at).total_seconds() > timeout:
            print("Too long, can't wait... Cancelling")
            client.cancel()
            break
    else:
        rv = await progress.get_result()

 
async def go_no_progress():
    client = AsyncClient('localhost')
    # Query is sent and result is fetched asynchronously. 
    rv = await client.execute('LONG AND COMPLICATED QUERY')

 
async def count():
    i = 0
    while True:
        print('Count', i)
        i += 1
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
    

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait([go_progress(), go_no_progress(), count()]))
```

Is this design good enough?

I should add that these issues are pretty fundamental with how the python kernel handles output. IIRC, there is a single datastructure containing metadata about where the output should go (e.g., the parent message, etc.), and that is shared across threads. So there's not an easy way to send a single output message with a piece of metadata saying "I'm from thread X" without constructing and sending the message yourself (in which case I suppose you could put in some metadata).

CC @minrk for verification of details about how output messages are sent from threads.

Dave Willmer did do some work on handling output from different threads at https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/pull/115, but in the end we decided to do output capturing in the browser side to support more kernels better.

Hello!

I'm afraid I have misled you and made this much more complicated than it has to be :-(. The code you used for a reference jumps through all kinds of hoops using ctypes, because it's trying to call `getsockopt` on a socket that it only has a fileno for, not a socket object. But on all the versions of Python we care about, we can create a socket object (maybe with incorrect values), and then we can directly use Python's regular `getsockopt` method and skip all that ctypes stuff. Also, the versions of Python we care about have the relevant `SO_WHATEVER` magic constants on the `socket` module on all the systems where they work at all, so we don't need to have the magic numbers like "39" for `SO_DOMAIN`. (And besides, those magic numbers are only correct for Linux.)

So my suggestion would be to delete all the ctypes stuff, and instead have a function like this, that takes whatever the user gave us, fixes it up as best we can (which might be more or less depending on the OS), and returns the fixed up values.

```python
def _fix_attrs_for_fileno(family, type, proto, fileno):
    # Wrap the raw fileno into a socket object
    sockobj = _stdlib_socket.socket(fileno=fileno)
    try:
        if hasattr(socket, "SO_DOMAIN"):
            family = sockobj.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_DOMAIN)
        if hasattr(socket, "SO_TYPE"):
            type = sockobj.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_TYPE)
        if hasattr(socket, "SO_PROTOCOL"):
            proto = sockobj.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_PROTOCOL)
    finally:
        # Unwrap it again, so that sockobj.__del__ doesn't try to close our socket
        sockobj.detach()
    return family, type, proto
```

And then in `fromfd` and `socket` we can do something like:

```python
family, type, proto = _fix_attrs_for_fileno(family, type, proto, fileno)
```

Does that make sense?

Our flags library assumes that your application does all imports at application startup time before `app.run(main)` is called rather than delayed dynamic imports at runtime.

In situations where there are potential delayed imports (some web frameworks seem to be a fan of this) we have suggested that people either:
   1. manually do all of the potential imports their server may do up front (ugh?) _or_
   2. at least create a single module of flag definitions that you expect all of those to need and always import that regardless of which subset of modules your server ends up actually importing later on.

@dzhulgakov @anderspapitto @linkerzhang This is not a fair comparison, over half of the code in the protobuf version is extracting inputs' shape info from the graph and at the end put the outputs value info back to the graph, and this is the in-place style I explicitly commented before as not preferred. These code that interact with the graph can be written once in the shape inference engine, and individual inference function will just take the NodeProto and a list of ValueInfoProto as parameters, and return a list of ValueInfoProto.
IMO for our needs the only thing we need to fix in the protobuf api is to provide a dict-lick interface for accessing attributes, and this can be done by adding some util functions like how caffe2 does https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/afe8f0997210694124fb349e2bf5b9878a987c35/caffe2/utils/proto_utils.h#L281.

At the least we'd need to handle splitting that back into
